text: BINTREE, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. W by N of Reepham. Area 2,120 acres. Pop. 409.
BINZEN, a town of Baden, in the Upper Rhine circle, 2 m. NW of Lorrach. Pop. 890. Muslin is manufactured here.

BIOBIO (R10), a river of Chili, which rises in the Andes, near the parallel of 38°, and flows WNW to the Pacific, which it enters after a course of 150 m., in which it passes Santa Fe and Conception. It is navigable for canoes a distance of 40 m. from its mouth. It forms the dividing line between the republic of Chili and the territory of the Araucanian CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CO Indians. Its chief affluents are the Laxa on the r., | and the Vergara on the l.

BIOCOVO, a mountain of Dalmatia, in the circle of Spalatro, at the foot of which the town of Macarsca is built. It is exceedingly steep, bare of trees and verdure, and covered with large stones and crags. The summit is composed of breccia, and a whitish limestone.—Fortis.
BIOCSKA, a town of Servia, in the prov. of Semendria, E of Usicza.
BIODOLA, a small rock in the bay of Prochio, on the N coast of the island of Elba.
BIOGLIO. See BIELLA.
BIOGRAD. See ZARA-VECCHIA.
BIOL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isére, cant. of Le Grands Lemps. Pop. 1,534.
BIOLLE (LA), a village of Sardinia, in the prov. of Savoy, 9 m. N of Chambery.
BIOLLET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Saint-Gervais. Pop. 1,154.
BION, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Mortain. Pop. 779.
BIORKO, an island in the gulf of Bothnia, off the coast of Sweden, in N lat. 59° 53'.—Also an island in the same gulf, off the coast of Finland, in N lat. 63° 21'.
BIORNEBORG, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Finland, at the embouchure of the Kumo, on the gulf of Finland, in N lat. 61° 29'. Pop. 4,570.
BIORNOE, an island off the W coast of Norway, in N lat. 60° 19'.
BIORNSHOLM, a town of Denmark, in the bailiwick of Aalborg, in N Jutland, on the Lymfiord, 30 m. SW of Aalborg. The largest market for wool in Jutland is held here.
BIORSATER, a village of Sweden, 6 m. from Mariestad. Pop. 1,100.
BIOT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. and 3 m. NNW of Antibes. Pop. 1,267.
BIOT (LE), a village of Sardinia, in the prov. of Chablais, 13 m. SE of Thonon. Pop. 800.
BIOUL, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, 8 m. NW of Dinant. It contains a fine castle, and possesses extensive lime-works. Pop. 913.
BIOULE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn-et-Garonne, cant. of Negrepelisse, on the r. bank of the Aveyron, 12 m. NE of Montauban. Pop. 1,251.

BIOZAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Alier, cant. of Gannat. Pop. 1,539. BIR, BIRA, or BEER, [Turkish BIREH-JIK,] a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pashalik of Diarmodzar, 39 m. direct or 60 m. travelling distance SW of Orfa; and 75 m. direct or 146 m. by road, NE of Aleppo. Pop. 4,000. It is situated on the side of a chalky hill descending steeply to the l. bank of the Euphrates, 313 m. by the stream above Anna, in the route of the caravans from Aleppo to Orfa and Diarbekir. The ancient walls still environ the town; but they, as well as the strong castle which formerly commanded the river, are rapidly falling into ruins. In the vicinity of the latter are a large caravanserai, and numerous grottoes which are used as lodging-places by travellers. There are also 5 mosques with tall minarets, a public bath, a khan, a few coffee-houses, and a small illbuilt bazaar. The number of houses was estimated by Niebuhr at 500; at the period of Buckingham's visit they did not exceed 400; but Colonel Chesney estimated them in 1831 at 1,800 or 2,000. Being built of chalk, the town is scarcely distinguishable from the rock on which it stands, but for the deep green of the cypresses and other trees which grow among the houses and relieve the eye. The inhabitants are two tribes of Turks, called Birk and Rashuan, who also extend 5 or 6 hours' distance along with wild rose, gooseberry, and other bushes. ^ BIR both banks of the river downwards, and 7 hours upwards. They are represented to be peaceful cultivators of the soil. At 14 hours from B. downwards the Arab tribes commence. Meat, fowls, bread, rice, wheat, eggs, milk, and vegetables, may be had from them at about a quarter or a third of English market prices; besides figs, pomegranates, apricots, melons, pears, and grapes.—This town, the ancient Birtha of Ptolemy, was, in 1109, taken by two Armenian princes from the Seljukian Turks. In 1117 it was captured by the crusading army of Baldwin, count of Edessa. Twenty years later it fell into the hands of the Atabecs of Syria, and ultimately became incorporated with the Ottoman empire. B., or rather the r. bank of the river opposite B., is the point at which the proposed steam-navigation of the Euphrates might, it is thought, advantageously commence, being the nearest point of the Euphrates to the Mediterranean: the distance by the most direct road from Bir to Scanderun is 120 m. From Bir to Bassora, the distance is 1,143 m., or from 5 to 7 days of calculated steam-voyage; while Bassora is 1,587 m., or 9 days' steaming from Bombay; and the river is navigable during the greater portion if not the whole of the year, for vessels of considerable burden. The average rate of its current is about 3 m. per hour. It is here 130 yards in breadth, and averages at low water a depth of 10 ft.
BIR, or BIRR, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Argau, in the bailiwick and 4 m. S of Brugg. It contains the ancient castle of Brunek.
BIRAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lotet-Garonne, cant. of Marmande. Pop. 1,239.
BIR-ALI, a Wahadi village at the bottom of a small bay on the S coast of Arabia, to the E of Jebel Ghorab, which is in N lat. 13° 59′ 20″, E long 48° 24½′.
BIR-AMBIR, a village of Arabia, in the line of route through the Desert from Cosseir to Keneh, 6 hours from Lagetta, and 31 hours from Keneh. It is the first village on the limits of cultivation.
BIRAN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Gers, cant. of Jegun, 11 m. NW of Auch. Pop. 1,336.

BIR-ANGLIS, some wells of brackish water in the Arabian desert, 21 hours WNW of Cosseir, which were dug by a detachment of the Anglo-Indian army when on their route into Egypt. They are 4 hours from Sayad Suliman.

BIRBHE' (EL), a village of Upper Egypt, on the E bank of the Nile, near the island of Phile, marking the boundary of Egypt in this quarter. BIRBHUM, [i. e., Vira-b'humi, or 'the Land of Heroes,'] a district of Hindostan, at the NW extremity of the prov. of Bengal, between N lat. 23° 25', and 24° 25'. Area 3,870 sq. m. Pop. in 1822, 1,267,065, of whom only 50,000 were Mahommedans. The country is mountainous, and in many parts uncultivated. Its chief productions are rice, sugar, and silk. Iron has been wrought, but not successfully. The chief towns are Suri, and Deoghur.
BIRCH, a township in the p. of Baschurch, in Salop. Pop. 22.—Also a township in the p. of Ellesmere, in Salop. Pop. with Lythe, 65.

BIRCH BAY, a bay on the W coast of North America, in the gulf of Georgia, in N lat. 48° 53', so called from the quantity of black birch-trees found growing on the coast. The SE part of this bay is formed by nearly perpendicular rocky cliffs, whence the higher woodland country retires a considerable distance to the NE, leaving an extensive space of low land between it and the sea, separated from the high ground by a rivulet of fresh water, which discharges itself at the bottom or N extremity of the bay. On the low land luxuriant grass is produced,
BIRCH (GREAT and LITTLE), two parishes in Essex, 5 m. SW of Colchester. Area 2,940 acres. Pop. 794.
BIRCH (LITTLE), a parish in Herefordshire, 4 m. S by E of Hereford. Area 930 acres. Pop. 375.
BIRCH (Much), a parish in Herefordshire, 61 m. S of Hereford. Area 1,330 acres. Pop. 484.
BIRCH POND, a township in Fayette co., in Tennessee, U. S., 199 m. SW by W of Nashville.
BIRCHALL, or BIRCHOTT, a parish in Kent, 4 m. E by S of Ashford. Area 310 acres. Pop. 37.
BIRCHAM (GREAT), a parish in Norfolk, 71 m. SW by S of Burnham-Westgate. Area 3,530 acres. Pop. 511.
BIRCHAM (Newton), a parish in Norfolk, 7 m. SW by S of Burnham-Westgate. Area 1,150 acres. Pop. 107.
BIRCHAM (Tofts), a parish in Norfolk, 7 m. SSW of Burnham - Westgate. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. 142.
BIRCHANGER, a parish in Essex, 2 m. SW by S of Stanstead - Mountfichet. Area 1,150 acres. Pop. 386.
BIRCHER, a township in Yarpole parish, in Herefordshire. Pop. 257.
BIRCHINGTON, a parish in Kent, 3½ m. W by S of Margate. Area 1,760 acres. Pop. 874.
BIRCHOVER, a chapelry in the p. of Youlgreave, in Derbyshire, 5 m. WNW of Matlock. Pop. 77.
BIR-DEVEDAR, a village of Egypt, 17 m. NE of Tineh.

BIRD ISLAND, a small island off the SW coast of Ireland, at the entrance into Dunmannus bay, co. Cork, in N lat. 51° 28'.—Also an islet in Strangford lough, on the coast of Ireland, 8 m. S of Newtown.

BIRD ISLAND, a small island in the Eastern seas, 6 m. N of Pulo Lucotta, in S lat. 3° 40', with a reef of rocks extending from the northern extremity. -Also an islet near the W coast of Sumatra, in N lat. 1° 39'.

BIRD ISLAND, an island in the South Pacific ocean, in S lat. 48° 17', about 4 m. in circumf., discovered by Cook in 1769.—Also an island, in S lat. 54°, near the NW coast of the island of New Georgia, discovered in 1775 by Cook.
BIRD ISLAND, a small island near the E coast of Newfoundland, 6 m. SE of Cape Bonavista.—Also a small island in the gulf of St. Lawrence, 63 m. W of Cape Anguilla on the island of Newfoundland.

BIRD ISLAND, a remarkable island in the North Pacific ocean, discovered in 1788 by the captain of the Prince of Wales, and visited in 1789 by Meares, and by Vancouver in 1794. It is a solitary rock, rising abruptly out of the immense ocean, in N lat. 23° 6'; and derives its name from the vast multitude of birds to which it affords asylum. It has the form of a saddle, high at each end, and low at the middle. Its greatest extent, which is in a direction S 74° W, and N 74° E, does not exceed 1 mile. Its northern, eastern, and western extremities, against which the sea breaks with great violence, rise perpendicularly from the ocean in lofty rugged cliffs, inaccessible but to its winged inhabitants; on its southern side the ascent is not so steep and abrupt; and near its western extremity is a small sandy beach, where, in fine weather and a smooth sea, a landing might probably be effected. At this place Vancouver saw some appearance of verdure, though it was destitute of tree or shrub; every other part was apparently without soil, and consisted only of naked rock. The Sandwich islanders recognise it under the appellation of Modoo Manoo, that is, 'Bird Island;' and from its great distance from all other land, and its proximity to their islands, it seems to claim to be ranked in the BIR group of the Sandwich islands, being 39 leagues N, and 51 W of Onehow.
BIRD ISLAND. See Aves.
BIRD ISLANDS. See AVES.
BIRD ISLANDS, a group of small rocky islands near Cape Padron, on the E coast of Africa.
BIRD'S NESTS, three rocks in the Eastern seas, about 25 ft. high, and covered with bushes, off the NW coast of the island of Tappa, in N lat. 6°, E long. 123° 35'.
BIRDBROOK, a parish in Essex, 7 m. NW of Castle-Hedingham. Area 2,640 acres. Pop. 557.
BIRDFORTH, a chapelry in Coxwold p., in the N. R. of Yorkshire. Area 540 acres. Pop. 44.
BIRDHAM, a parish in Sussex, 4 m. SW by S of Chichester. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 506.
BIRDINGBURY, a parish in Warwickshire, 5 m. N by E of Southam. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. 201.
BIRDSALL, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. SSE of New Malton. Area 3,650 acres. Pop. 267.—Also a township in Alleghany co., in the state of New York, U.S., 325 m. W by S of Albany. Pop. 328.
BIRDSVILLE, a township in Burke co., in the state of Georgia, U. S., 74 m. E of Milledgeville.
BIR-EL-MALHA, a salt spring 180 m. SW of Dongola, which serves as a station for the caravans from Cairo to Darfur. Natron is found in its vicinity.

BIRGHAM, a small village on the N bank of the Tweed, in the p. of Eccles, in Berwickshire, immediately opposite Carham, in Northumberland. It is of some historical celebrity in the annals of both kingdoms.
BIRIUSA, a river of Asiatic Russia, rising in the district of Nishni-Udinsk, in the gov. of Irkutsk, and joining the Tchuna, in the gov. of Tomsk, after a course of 300 m. from S to N.
BIRIUTSCH, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Voronetz, on the l. bank of the Sosna, 90 m. SSW of Voronetz. Pop. 6,000.
BIRJE, or BIRZE, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Wilna, 35 m. NNE of Ponievieje. Pop. 2,000.
BIRKBECK-FELLS, a township in Westmoreland, in the parishes of Orton and Crosby-Ravensworth. Pop. 200. The fell is a ramification of the Cambrian range of mountains crossing the head of Borrowdale.

BIRKBY, a township in Canonby-cross p., in Cumberland. Pop. 89.—Also a township in Muncaster p., in the same co. Pop. 119.—Also a p. in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. NW of Northallerton. Area 3,400 acres. Pop. 256, of whom 74 were in the township of B.
BIRKDALE, a township in North Meols p., in Lancashire, 7 m. NW of Ormskirk. Area 2,130 acres. Pop. 557.
BIRKENDORF, a village of Baden, in the Lake circle, 6 m. SSW of Bonndorf. Pop. 522.

BIRKENFELD, a principality in Oldenburg, comprised between the Prussian prov. of the Rhine, which bounds it on the NW and SW, and the principality of Meisenheim, which bounds it on the E and SE. Its length from NE to SW is 27 m.; its breadth from NW to SE 10 m. The superficies is nearly 180 sq. m. It is divided into the 3 amts or bailiwicks of B., Nohfelden, and Oberstein, which are subdivided into 9 communes, and comprise 1 city, 1 town, 62 villages. Pop. in 1837, 17,150. This principality is mountainous in the W part. The Nahe waters the S part, and forms the E limit from Wierbach to Kirn. Hops and flax are grown; and there is a considerable quantity of timbered land. Cattle and bees are extensively reared.—This principality, which made part of the French dep. of the Sarre, was given to the duke of Oldenburg in 1815 by the treaty of Vienna.

BIRKENFELD, the cap. of the above principality, is situated on the banks of a rivulet which falls into the Nahe 25 m. E of Treves. Pop. 1,800. It is the seat of administration, of a chamber of finance, and of the superintendancy of the Lutheran church. It has a castle, a Catholic and a Lutheran church; and possesses manufactories of cloth. Great cattle-fairs are held here.
BIRKENFELD, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Black forest, bailiwick of Neuenburg. Pop. 1,012.

BIRKENHEAD, a township and chapelry in the p. of Bidstone in Cheshire, on the W shore of the Mersey, opposite Liverpool. Pop. in 1801, 110; in 1831, 236; in 1841, 8,227. In 1818 there were only 3 houses, besides the priory, and a few cottages; in 1831 there were 396; in 1841, 1,256. Area in 1844, including Claughton, and part of Oxon, 1,388 acres. A. P. in 1815, £1,732; in 1841, £39,570; and, in 1843-4, of the enlarged township, £64,481. The town of B., which has suddenly sprung into existence, promises, in a few years, to be one of the most handsome as well as the most important seaports in the kingdom. The affairs of the township are managed by a board of commissioners, 25 in number, 3 of whom are appointed by the town-council of Liverpool, and the remainder elected by the rate-payers. The township has bought both the Woodside and the Monk's ferry. Extensive docks are now forming here. They comprise a tidal basin of 16 acres; a low water basin of 37 acres; and a floating dock of 150 acres, which, it is intended, shall be surrounded with warehouses on the plan of those at St. Katharine's dock, London, and are placed in communication with the Chester and Birkenhead railway by means of a tunnel under the town. The Chester Junction railway is at this place carried through a tunnel 324 yards in length, which brings the terminus of the line almost to the water's edge at the Monk's ferry pier. This railway is 145 m. in length. A railway to connect Manchester, Warrington, and Runcorn, with B.; and another to connect it by the Chester line, with Shrewsbury, are projected. B. is one of the polling-places for the members for South Chester.

BIRKER, a township in Millom p., in Cumberland, 6 m. E by N of Ravenglass. Pop., with Austhwaite, 105. BIRKET-EL-HADJ, [i. e. 'Lake of the Pilgrims,'] a spot 5 m. to the E of Matarieh, and 12 m. NE of Cairo, in Lower Egypt, which forms the rendezvous of the Mecca caravan.
BIRKET-EL-KERUN. See MŒRIS.
BIRKET-EL-MARIUT. See MAREOTIS.
BIRKIN, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. NE of Ferrybridge. Area 5,890 acres. Pop. 921, of whom 169 were in the township of B.
BIRKSTEIN, or BURGSTEIN, a village of Bohemia, in the circle and 30 m. SE of Leitmeritz, on the Rodowitz. Pop. 944. It is celebrated for its glass and crystal manufactories.

BIRLAT, a river of Moldavia, which rises 9 m. S of Jassy, and flowing from N to S, joins the Sereth 27 m. NW of Galatz, after a course of 96 m.—Also a town of Moldavia, on the r. bank of the B., 36 m. WSW of Falczin.
BIRLEY, a parish in Hereford, 4 m. E by N of Weobley. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 172.
BIRLING, a parish in Kent, 6 m. NW of Maidstone. Area 2,240 acres. Pop. 511.—Also a township in Warkworth p., in Northumberland. Pop. 80.
BIRLINGHAM, a parish in Worcestershire, 3 m. SSW of Pershore, on the N bank of the Avon. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. 390. BIRMAN EMPIRE.

THE BIRMAN EMPIRE. The first independent state which we meet in proceeding eastwards from Hindostan, or in India beyond the Ganges, is Birmah, which comprises the ancient kingdoms of Ava and Pegu, and until recently comprised that of Aracan. By far the greater portion of this extensive country is still a terra incognita to geographers. A few points of the coast, -two or three of the numerous islands scattered along it, in the bay of Bengal, -together with a narrow tract of country on either side of the Irawaddi, from the gulf of Martaban to the city of Amarapura,-are almost the only parts of this once powerful empire of which we can be said to possess any knowledge. Colonel Symes-who was employed in an embassy to the Birman court-supposed, from the information of the natives, that the B. empire, previous to the late war with the East-India company, and its consequent dismemberment, extended from 9° to 26° N lat., and from 92° to 104° E long., its length being thus 1,050 Geog. m., and its breadth 600 Geog. m. Taken in its most extensive sense, that is, including all the countries subject to its influence. Hamilton supposed that the Birman dominion, previous to the late war, might contain 194,000 English sq. m. Since both these authors first wrote, however, the kingdom of Aracan, the province of Tenasserim, and the districts of Ye, Tavoy, and Mergui, amounting, it is supposed, to 51,000 sq. m., have been wrested from this extensive native power by the East India company; and its southern limits have receded from 9° to 15° 45' N lat. Its extreme N limit certainly reaches the parallel of 27°; but its extreme E limit cannot be carried beyond the meridian of 100°. Boundaries.] The Birman empire is bounded on the N by Assam and Tibet; on the NE and E by China, Laos, and Cambodia; on the S by Siam, Tavoy, and Aracan; and on the W by Aracan, Chittagong, and Munipore, which are now interposed between its W frontiers and the E frontiers of Bengal. Divisions and General Physical features.] The southern, or best known portion of this country, is a low, level land, which, like the plains of Bengal, and the delta of the Nile, is annually inundated by the rivers. All the country to the S of Prome, in N lat. 18° 50', formerly constituted the kingdom of Pegu. The central provinces consist of a succession of hills of moderate elevation, divided by fertile and well-wooded valleys; while the northern parts of the country, approaching Tibet, tower into mountains of from 6,000 to 10,000 ft. A large proportion of the empire is covered with forests in which the wild elephant and the tiger are sovereigns. Interspersed between these vast and impenetrable woods, and among the windings of the wild and lofty hills, are innumerable lakes, many of them so large as rather to deserve the name of inland seas, which form the haunts of immense flocks of aquatic birds, and abound in various species of fish. The coast is low and marshy, and broken or indented by numerous arms of the sea, or small bays; but there are only three harbours now belonging to B., those, namely, of Martaban, Rangoon, and Bassein. The extent of coast-line round the gulf of Martaban is about 250 m.—The Irawaddi divides the original territory of the Birmahs into two unequal parts. To the E of that river they possess a tract of ten days' journey, or about 150 m. in breadth, to the banks of a stream called the Salong-Miet, which falls into the Sittong, a river which has its embouchure in the gulf of Martaban. These two rivers at present form the boundaries with Siam; and their banks on both sides are waste and deserted on account of the continual predatory warfare between the two countries. A ridge of high mountains divides the basin of the Sittong from that of the Irawaddi; and the country on the E side of that great river is, for the most part, barren and jungly, and inhabited by wild tribes, whose fierceness and independence increase as they advance towards the high regions of the N. To the W of the Irawaddi, the Birman territory extends from 10 to 30 m. from the river, to the base of the mountains which separate it from Aracan. This tract expands as it runs N; but becomes gradually more rude and mountainous, until it presents the appearance of an uncultivated desert. The plains of Manchewban, between the rivers Irawaddi and Chedouwain are, however, reputed to form the granary of B. Beyond a range of mountains to the W of the Chedouwain is the country generally called Cossay or Cossyah, inhabited by a handsome, muscular race of men, who are governed by their own rajahs or chiefs, and appear to be nearly independent of Birman control. The districts on the NE, on the frontier of China, are likewise inhabited by independent tribes, who appear to receive the general designation of Shans and Singfos; but the navigation of the Irawaddi as far as Quantong is in the hands of the Birmese, and no part of the Chinese empire extends so far W as the banks of the Irawaddi. Mountains.] The Anapectumew range, sometimes called Yeomadong, beginning at Cape Negrais on the gulf of Martaban, divides the delta of the Irawaddi from the waters flowing westwards to the gulf of Bengal; and the now British territory of Aracan from Birmah. To the N of the parallel of 21° these mountains branch out in various directions; but little is known of their different ranges. Three passes lead from Aracan across the Anapectumew into the B. territories. — Another mountain-range, running N and S, appears to divide the basin of the Irawaddi from that of the Saluen, but little is known of its configuration. Another great range, running N and S, separates the basin of the Saluen from that of the Meinam, and stretches along the peninsula of Malacca.—The Langtang mountains divide the upper tributaries of the Irawaddi from the valley of the Upper Brahmaputra. Rivers.] The great river of B. is the Irawaddi, which, rising in Tibet, near the sources of the Brahmaputra, runs in a prevailingly S course throughout the entire length of the Birman dominions to the gulf of Martaban. At Manchi, a town of the Bor Kamti, this river is 80 yards broad, and fordable at its ordinary level, and has an alt. of 1,855 ft. above sea-level. From Manchi to Bhanmo, in N lat. 24° 7', it falls 1,300 ft. in 350 m. The breadth of the river at Amarapura, in N lat. 21° 55', is 2 m., and here the river flows with a gentle current through a rich plain, gradually expanding towards the S. Below Ava, the river attains a breadth of 4 m. Under the parallel of 18° N lat., it begins to divide into branches, some of which reunite again; and it reaches the sea by 14 different branches.—The principal tributary of the Irawaddi is the Kyan-Duen or Khyen-Dwen, which in the upper part of its course is known as the Ningthi. This river, descending from the mountains on the confines of Assam, flows through a fertile country, and joins the Irawaddi, on the r. bank, with a volume of water nearly 1 m. in breadth, 5 m. above Tirrupmiu, in N lat. 21° 40'.—The next important tributary of the Irawaddi is the Myitnge, which rises in the Chinese prov. of Yunnan; and after a course of nearly 400 m. to the W of S, enters the Irawaddi at Ava.—The Saluen or Kaluen, rises in Tibet, flows through Yunnan, divides Upper Laos, Siam, and 30 BIRMAN EMPIRE. Martaban, on the E, from the Birman territories, and flows into the gulf of Martaban. Climate, Soil, and Productions.] The climate of this country generally resembles that of Hindostan, though it is represented as being more temperate and salubrious. Extremes of heat and cold are seldom experienced; the seasons are regular. In Pegu there are only two seasons, the wet and the dry; and the therm. ranges from 50° to 90°. In the valley of the Irawaddi, three seasons are marked,the cold, the hot, and the rainy. The soil of the upper provinces, in general is a sandy loam, on a bed of freestone; in the lower provinces, it is deeper, and has a larger proportion of argillaceous earth and vegetable matter. According to Symes, the southern provinces exhibit a vegetation no less luxuriant than that of Bengal. Rice, and the various kinds of grain which are cultivated in Hindostan, are here produced in abundance; but the arts of husbandry are still very rude. The plough is nothing more than a large wooden rake on which the ploughman stands while he drives the oxen or buffaloes that draw it. Wheat is plentiful and of a good quality. Esculent vegetables form a considerable portion of the food of the inhabitants. All the tropical fruits are produced in perfection; and tobacco of an excellent quality, sugarcanes, cotton, indigo, and cossumba, a beautiful red dye. In the upper provinces wheat, and all the variety of pulse and grain known in Indian agriculture, are grown. The spontaneous produce of the forests is of superior quality. Oak is said to be unknown in the eastern districts; but its place is advantageously supplied by the teak, celebrated for its durability, and its fitness for the construction of ships, and which has for many years supplied materials for the dock-yards of Rangoon, Madras, and Calcutta. The fir is found in the northern districts. The catechu, the white sandal-tree, the ebony-tree, the sycamore-fig, the Indian fig, the banyan-tree, several kinds of palms, and many other trees of which the wood is used for various purposes, may be added to the list. Among plants useful in medicine, or in the arts, are ginger and cardamum, turmeric, betel-pepper, black pepper, and long pepper, with several kinds of capsicum. The Justicia tinctoria dyes green; the morinda, the gamboge, and carthamus, yield a yellow tinge. The tamarind, the aloe, and the camphor-tree, are all products of the B. territories; as well as the cinnamon, laurel, nutmeg, spikenard, and bamboo. The plantain, the cocoa-nut, and the sago-palm, are indigenous. Vines are found in the forests; and though they are at present inferior to those of the south of Europe, it may be presumed that this inferiority is owing to the want of cultivation. Among the fruits may be enumerated the mango, the pine-apple, the mangosteen-plum, custard-apple, papaw fig, lemon, lime, and orange. The species of flowers are said to be very numerous and diversified; and many of them are described as joining the greatest beauty of colour to the utmost fragrancy of smell. Animals. The animal kingdom in B. presents the same species which are found in Hindostan. The horses are of a small size, but vigorous and spirited. Elephants roam in every part of the country, but chiefly in Pegu. The wild boar, tiger, and leopard, inhabit the jungles. The ichneumon, or rat of Pharaoh, is said to be in a great measure peculiar to this country. Buffaloes are numerous, but their flesh is never used by the inhabitants as food, nor indeed is any other kind of flesh, except that of game. Poultry is abundant. There are several species of poisonous snakes; and leeches abound in the marshes. Minerals.] Besides tin, iron, antimony, lead, arsenic, and sulphur, the B. territories afford very pure amber, and several kinds of precious stones, among which may be reckoned amethysts, garnets, jasper, sapphires, and rubies. The ruby is said to be almost as peculiar to the B. territories as the true diamond is to Hindostan. In Pegu, gold is found in the sand of the rivers, and in some places it is dug from mines; it has even been supposed by some geographers that this country is the Golden Chersonesus of the ancients. Symes assures us, that, in a mountain called Woobelutaun, near the river Khyen-Dwen, there are mines in which gold, silver, rubies, and sapphires, are all wrought at the same time. The marble dug from the quarries a few miles from Amarapura is reckoned equal to the finest Italian; but it is monopolized by government, and held sacred because the images of Godama are chiefly composed of this material. On the E bank of the Irawaddi, near Renankeang, in N lat. 20° 30', scattered over a space of 16 sq. m., are the celebrated wells which yield petroleum oil, a species of coal-tar, also monopolized by government, and from which a large revenue is derived; the annual average produce being estimated at 92,781 tons, valued at nearly £1,000,000. Coal is said to be plentiful; and nitre, natron, and salt, are procured in the upper provinces. Population.] The population of Birmah can only be conjecturally estimated. "Of the pop. of the B. dominions," says Symes, "I could only form a conclusion from the information I received of the number of cities, towns, and villages in the empire; these, I was assured by a person who might be supposed to know, and had no motive for deceiving me, amount to 8,000, not including the [then] recent addition of Aracan. If this be true, which I have no reason to doubt, and we suppose each town, on an average, to contain 800 houses, and each house 6 persons, the result will determine the pop. at 14,400,000. Few of the inhabitants live in solitary habitations; they mostly form themselves into small societies, and their dwellings, thus collected, compose their ruas or villages; if, therefore, we reckon their number, including Aracan, at 17,000,000, the calculation may not be widely erroneous,—I believe it rather falls short than exceeds the truth." Captain Cox, who succeeded Colonel Symes as ambassador, reduced these 17,000,000, to 8,000,000; while Captain Canning, who visited the country in 1810, and with whom Hamilton agrees, brings down the number to 3,000,000. The first of these estimates is certainly exaggerated, and the last probably much underrated. Without including the inhabitants of Aracan, and the other ceded provinces—which were, of course, comprehended in Captain Canning's calculation—Crawfurd estimates the pop. at 4,000,000, which gives about 22 to the sq. m.; and Captain Trant concludes, from the amount and proportion of the military conscriptions, and from personal view of the districts through which he passed, that the whole pop. of the empire probably amounts to 6,000,000; and shows how this calculation may be reconciled with the estimate of Symes, "Colonel Symes, in his work on Ava," says Trant, "computes the pop. at 17,000,000, by supposing the number of towns and villages to amount to 8,000; but of this number, if we may judge by experience, more than twothirds only nominally existed, the inhabitants having probably emigrated to other spots, to which a new name would be given, whilst the deserted villages, in the returns of the district, would be placed on the same list with the inhabited ones. This I have remarked in many instances to be the case." In connexion with this fact of the immense number of ruined and deserted towns, both in Pegu and Ava Proper, Trant gives a number of highly interesting notices of the architectural remains and other antiquities of the country. From these monuments, it would appear that this country must have been, at one time, the seat of an empire far more populous, better skilled in the arts of civilization, and altogether more flourishing and wealthy, than the present. The more modern tale of desolation is told as well by the traces of villages thickly strewed over the country, where the nearest hamlets are now 10 m. asunder, as by the ruined ramperts and vast solitary area of Pegu, Pagham-mew, Prome, and various other once magnificent cities. The surviving monuments of earlier ages are still more remarkable. "The neatly and strongly arched roofs of old temples show a perfect acquaintance with the art of vaulting, which the B. have now totally lost. Frescoes of great age are found on the walls of these structures, still retaining the most brilliant colouring, and in every respect superior to more modern attempts of the kind; and those enormous masses of brick, the Dagon and Shoemadoo, and Shoezeegan pagodas, at Rangoon, Pegu, and Pagahm, are of immemorial antiquity. It is remarkable that these stupendous constructions are altogether dissimilar from any religious edifices in India, and 'approach,' says Trant, 'in idea nearer to the pyramids than any other relic of antiquity." The pop., inconsiderable as it is for so extensive a territory, is far from being a homogeneous mass, distinguished by the same manners, language, and religion. It consists, according to Craw- BIRMAN EMPIRE. furd, of at least 18 distinct tribes, differing from each other in many of those great physical and moral features which constitute a wall of separation, as it were, among men. The Birmans Proper occupy the central district of the empire from the parallel of 18° to 23°, and from the Aracan frontier to the Saluen river. The Shans are spread over all the N districts to the frontiers of China. The Singfu are chiefly located in the extreme NW on the frontiers of Assam. The Peguans-called Talains by the B.-are nearly confined to the delta of the Irawaddi and the lower course of the Saluen. Blumenbach and Virey have classed the Birmans with the Mongols; Bory de St. Vincent, with the Chinese; while Crawfurd-whose authority is of great weight-considers them to be more nearly allied to the Malays. In Ava, the Birmans Proper are at once the most numerous and the most civilized; and each nation, in proportion to the smallness of its numbers, recedes further and further from the point of refinement attained by the leading tribe. But however much these tribes may differ from each other in language, religion, and refinement, they have all, according to Crawfurd, the same physical type, that is, the same cast of features, the same complexion, and the same form. The physical characteristics of the B. are those of the Hindu-Chinese family. Their complexion is olive; their hair black, coarse, and lank; their face is broad at the cheek bones, but narrow at the forehead and chin, and the general expression is rather lively and pleasing. "The Burmahs," says a writer in the Bengal Hurkaru, "are, in general, men of low stature, but stout, muscular, and capable of enduring great privation. They are possessed of amazing activity and strength, and the postures into which a Burmah throws himself, while engaged in pugilistic and other athletic exercises are scarcely to credited. In what we call bottom, they are not inferior to Englishmen, and they resemble us likewise in their love of boxing, and similar amusements. In true bravery they take the lead of all other Asiatics, as no one who has ever had an opportunity of seeing them at Rangoon will be disposed to deny." Captain Franklin gives a less favourable sketch of the native B.; describing them as, to their superiors, abjectly submissive; in power, rapacious and cruel; in war, treacherous and ferocious; in their dealings, litigious and faithless; in appetite, insatiable and avaricious; in habit, lazy; and in their ideas, persons, houses, and food, "obscenely filthy below any thing that has claims to humanity!" Father Sangermanno also describes them as "servile, slothful, and false." The principal part of the male dress,—which covers the loins, and reaches half-way down the leg,-consists of a double piece of cloth about 10 cubits long, loosely wrapped about the body. Over this a frock is worn, with sleeves open in front, and reaching below the knees: this frock consists in summer of white cotton, broad-cloth, or velvet; and in winter is quilted. The head is covered with a small square handkerchief, commonly of English book-muslin, which is worn like a turban. The lower classes of women wear only a single garment, called a thabi, and resembling a sheet. This is wrapped round the body, and crossing the breasts, is fastened under the arms. It descends almost to the ankles, but is not closed by a seam before, so that—like the Spartan females, if Euripides belie them not—when walking, a great part of the leg is exposed. From this habit custom has long removed every idea of indelicacy. Among the upper ranks, sumptuary laws are established, which determine the several ornaments and decorations by which the different classes are distinguished. The men wear ear-rings, which, in those of high rank, are generally of a large size, and weigh down the ears almost to the shoulders. The rank of women is distinguished by the fillets and ornaments by which the hair is bound in a bunch on the top of the head. Both sexes wear the hair long; the men tying it in a knot on the crown of the head, the women on the back. The custom of tattooing universally prevails among the male part of the population. The tattooed figures are of a black or blue colour, upon a brown ground; and are produced by a kind of lamp-black procured from the soot of sesamum oil mixed with the gall of the mirga fish. The Siamese regard this practice of tattooing as barbarous, probably because it is the national custom of their ancient enemies. Few of the nations beyond the Brahmaputra except the B. and Peguans, have preserved this ancient custom. The practice of chewing betel is universal, and the size and fabric of the paun-box, denote the rank of the owner. The B. are untainted with that jealousy of disposition which pervades so many Eastern nations, and which has led to the careful seclusion of the women from general society. Polygamy is prohibited, except to the royal family; but, though a man can only have one wife, he may have as many concubines as he chooses; and divorces are obtained with great facility. If the latter live in the same house with the wife, they are bound, by law, to perform for her every office of a servant; they attend her when she goes abroad; bear her fan, her betel-box, her waterflagon; and thus, by contributing to her ease, pomp, and pride, compensate in some degree for the share which they may attract of her husband's affection. The B. funerals are conducted with considerable solemnity. The bodies of those of the higher ranks are burnt; those of the lower ranks, and all who die under the age of 15, are thrown into a river, or buried. Religion.] In religion the more civilized tribes of this empire are Buddhists; but they do not acknowledge the elder Buddha, or Rama, the emperor of Ceylon. Their legislator is the younger Buddha, or Bhaddu of the Hindus, the ninth incarnation of Vishnu. They admit a metempsychosis or transmigration after death; but maintain that the soul perishes with the body, and that from this complete dissolution another individual springs, which will be man, or beast, or nadt,—a being superior to man,— according to the merits or demerits of its predecessor. At last, after a successive series of dissolutions and regenerations, all beings attain the state of niban or nerwan,—a state of ecstasy, or of perfect abstraction from all sensation, thought, or desire.—The temples are usually in the form of a pyramid, and some of them are of great height. Besides images of Godama, the temples contain the images of the favourites of their god, with representations of animals, such as elephants, monkeys, and lions. The votaries of Godama say prayers at sunrise, and before they retire to rest; and they make use of rosaries formed of various kinds of seeds. The public acts of religion are reserved for particular solemnities, such as the dedication of a temple, or the celebration of an annual festival. Offerings are made; but the religion of Buddha forbids to sacrifice any animal. The offerings consist of fruits, boiled rice, and flowers, with various figures of paper and gold-leaf. The rich present white umbrellas, with ornaments of gold, slippers, canes, pillows, and gilded utensils; the poor, who cannot afford offerings so expensive, present imitations of them in paper. To gild part of a temple is considered a very meritorious act. For three months of the year, a kind of lent is observed, during which the more scrupulous fast from sunrise to sunset. At the conclusion of this period, a whole month is celebrated as a kind of festival. The rahans, or the priests of Godama, do not appear to take any part in the religious acts performed in the temples; few of them, indeed, are present at processions, or at religious ceremonies of any kind. They live together in colleges, or convents, founded for that purpose by the munificence of the rich. The decency of their lives, and their hospitality to strangers, are said to be eminent; and the respect paid to them by the other classes of the community is considerable. No rahan ever kindles a fire, lest by that act he deprive an animal of life; he consequently dresses no victuals, but depends upon receiving them in that state from the charity of others. He is allowed, however, to eat every kind of food which may be presented; even animal food is not forbidden to him. Every rahan is, by his religious profession, bound to procure his food by "the labour of his feet," as they term it. In the morning, as soon as he can distinguish the veins on his hands, he issues from his convent, with his sabeit, or vessel of wicker-work, under his arm. Stopping at every door in his way, but observing the deepest silence, he receives into his sabiet such ready-dressed provisions as the inhabitants think proper to afford, and passes on without returning thanks. He is forbidden to use solicitations; and is not permitted even to give notice of his being at the door. The provisions thus obtained, after satisfying the immediate necessities of the collector, are given to the poor, or to the needy stranger. In every convent of rahans there is a superior called zara, or 'reader,' who is supposed to enjoy considerable power among those of his own convent, as they always approach him with respect. The zaras of the principal convents live in splendid apartments, have numerous attendants, and enjoy several privileges denied to the principal nobles. Their authority, however, does not extend beyond their own colleges. Before a person be admitted into the priesthood, and received into a convent of rahans, he undergoes a public examination, to discover whether or not he be free of bodily infirmities. The initiation is pompous, and expensive; but a person who has attached himself to this profession, is not supposed to be bound to it for life: he may, when he pleases, without scandal, quit his convent, and engage in the active concerns of life. The kioums, or convents of the rahans, are different in their structure from common houses, and resemble the architecture of the Chinese. Convents of young women are said formerly to have existed, into which virgins entered, making a vow of perpetual celibacy. The White elephant.] Besides these wholly religious characters, there is a very important personage, the second dignitary in the kingdom,—the White elephant, who has a regular cabinet, composed of ministers, secretaries, under-secretaries, &c., &c. This animal is not, as has been supposed, an object of worship among the B., but merely forms an indispensable part of the regalia of sovereignty. The title of 'Lord of the White elephant,' or rightful possessor of an incarnate symbol of Buddha, confers actual supremacy in the Buddhic states. The residence of the White elephant is contiguous to the royal palace, with which it is connected by a long open gallery, supported by numerous wooden pillars, at the farther end of which a curtain of black velvet embossed with gold, conceals the august animal from the eyes of the vulgar; and before this curtain, the offerings intended for him are displayed. His trappings are of gold, studded with large diamonds, pearls, sapphires, rubies, and other precious stones; and his betel-box, ankle-rings, and the vessel out of which he feeds, are all of pure gold. The elephant, thus treated, appears to be an animal whose colour has been changed by a disease of the leprous kind; but is by the Birmans supposed to contain a human soul, in the last stage of many millions of transmigrations, and about to be absorbed into the essence of the Deity. Language and Writing.] The language of the B. has 33 simple sounds or characters; and is, like the languages of Europe, written from left to right. Its structure is very inartificial; and there are no inflexions; the relations of number, mode, and time, being indicated by prefixing or affixing certain particles. The Pali language, written in the Sanscrit alphabet, is the sacred text of Ava, Pegu, and Siam; but in a specimen of the Lord's prayer in that dialect, the missionaries could only discover three Sanscrit words. A part seems taken from the Chinese. The character in common use is a round Nagari, derived from the Pali, which is square. It is formed of circles, and segments of circles, variously disposed, and written from left to right. The Baptist missionaries have translated the Scriptures into the Pali. In writing, numerous contractions are used, and the whole is executed with great despatch. The zares, or writers in courts, often write down what an officer dictates who does not speak slow. Almost every B. carries with him a parawaik, consisting of a sheet of strong blackened paper, measuring from 4 to 8 ft. long, and about 18 inches wide. It is folded up somewhat in the form of a fan; the breadth of each fold being about 6 inches, and the length corresponding to the breadth of the sheet. Upon this parawaik, with a pencil of steatites, he keeps his accounts and memorandums. Books in the Pali text are sometimes composed of thin stripes of bamboo, delicately plaited and varnished over in such a manner as to form a smooth and hard surface, on a leaf of any dimensions. This surface is afterwards gilded, and the sacred letters are traced upon it in black and shining japan; the margin being illuminated by wreaths and figures of gold, on a red, green, or black ground. In every monastery there is a repository of books, which are usually kept in lacquered chests. When at the capital, Colonel Symes paid a visit to the royal library, of which he gives us the following interesting description. "It is," says he, "a large brick building, raised on a terrace, and covered by a roof of a very compound structure. It consists of one square room, with an enclosed virando, or gallery, surrounding it. The room was locked, and, as we had not a special order for seeing it, the person who had the care of the library said that he was not at liberty to open the doors, but assured us there was nothing in the inside different from what we might see in the virando, where a number of large chests, curiously ornamented with gilding and japan, were ranged in regular order against the wall. I counted fifty; but there were many more, probably not less than a hundred. The books were regularly classed, and the contents of each chest were written in gold letters on the lid. The librarian opened two, and showed me some very beautiful writing, on thin leaves of ivery, the margins of which were ornamented with flowers of gold, neatly executed. I saw also some books written in the ancient Pali, the religious text. Every thing seemed to be arranged with perfect regularity; and I was informed that there were books on divers subjects; more on divinity than any other; but history, music, medicine, painting, and romance, had their separate treatises. The volumes were disposed under distinct heads, regularly numbered; and if all the other chests were as well-filled as those that were submitted to our inspection, it is not improbable that his Birman majesty may possess a more numerous library than any potentate from the banks of the Danube to the borders of China," The B. are said to possess several historical works, containing an account of the lives and actions of the different families of their princes; but they are fabulous, and abound with omens and prodigies. They have also translations of the history of China and Siam, and of the kingdoms of Kathee, Ko-shampyee, Pegu, Saymmay, and Layuzayu. In scientific pursuits, the B. are far behind the Hindus. Their astronomy is very defective, and their geography still more so. The Birman year commences on the 18th of April. The common year is lunar; but they are likewise acquainted with the solar year of 365 days. The year is divided into 12 months, alternately consisting of 30 days and 29 days; and is consequently 11 days shorter than the solar year. In order to bring it nearly to coincide with the solar year, an intercalary month is added every third year. This, however, does not make the coincidence complete, and consequently the principal festivals revolve round the whole months. The skill of their surgeons extends only to the dressing of wounds, and the setting of bones. In medicine, the Birmans have several books. In law, the B. have numerous treatises, particularly the Institutes of Menu, and copious commentaries upon them. The king who sat on the throne when the British embassy under Symes was sent to this country, had caused the Institutes of Menu to be translated from the English of Sir W. Jones. The B, are fond of poetry and music. They have epic as well as religious poems of high celebrity; and they are fond of reciting, in heroic numbers, the exploits of their kings and generals. It is said that the prowess of the great Alompra, the deliverer of his country, is celebrated in verses net unworthy of his courage and his fortune. The members of the British embassy saw one of their dramatic representations at Pegu, and gave it considerable praise. The dialogue was spirited, without rant; the action animated, without being extravagant; and the dresses of the principal performers were showy, yet becoming. Music is a science which is also held in considerable estimation throughout the B. empire, and is cultivated more generally than in India. Arts and Manufactures. In the fine arts, the B. cannot be expected to have made any considerable progress. Their architecture is greatly inferior to that of the Hindus and the Chinese: the few respectable pagodas which are found in the country are constructed after Siamese models. Their paintings are unintelligible unless accompanied by written descriptions. Crawfurd says that, as far as he could discover, the art of casting images of metal-which is daily practised by the Siamese-was unknown to the B. In the useful arts, they are scarcely more advanced. The women are the only manufacturers in Ava, if we except a few male captives of the Cassay nation, who earn their livelihood by weaving. The B. female weavers produce good cotton-fabrics; but the artisans of Manchester undersell them even in the interior of the country. The principal silk manufactories are supplied with the raw material from China and Pegu; and the artisans in this branch of industry also are women. They dye their silks very well in various brilliant colours. The common, coarse, unglazed earthenware of the B. is the best in India, and is very cheap; the wood and ivory turnery-ware is also very good. Iron-ore is melted in Ava, where there are manufactures in which swords, spears, muskets, or rather matchlocks, knives, scissors, and carpenters' tools are fabricated. All articles of steel are imported from Bengal. Crawfurd saw in the market of Ava-without being able to ascertain the uses to which it is applied—considerable quantities of antimony reduced to the metallic state. At a certain village Colonel Symes saw thirty or forty large yards crowded with statuaries at work on images of various sizes, but all of the same personage, namely Godama, sitting cross-legged on a pedestal. The quarries from which they obtained the materials were only a few miles distant; and the marble was brought to the village in blocks. The smallest image exceeded the human stature, and the price was said to be 100 tikals, or about £12 or £13. The workmen's only tools were a chisel and a mallet, and they smoothed the images with freestone and water. Many were beautifully polished, presenting a transparent clearness far surpassing the brightest polish that European marble ever exhibits. Of late years, the Birmans have made rapid progress in the art of ship-building. Formerly they used only small vessels like boats; but, in consequence of their communication with Europeans, they are now launching vessels of considerable magnitude. At Rangoon, Colonel Symes saw several ships on the stocks from 600 to 1,000 tons burden; three or four of these vessels belonged to English adventurers. Ships may be constructed in this country at one-third less than in the Ganges, and for nearly one-half of what they would cost at Bombay. The Birman shipwrights appear to finish their work well. Commerce.] The natural products of the B. empire, which are articles of exportation, or likely to become so, are the following: rice, grain, cotton, indigo, cardamums, black pepper, aloes, sugar, saltpetre, salt, teak-timber, stick-lac, kuth or Terra Japonica, areca nuts, dammer, fustic, sapan-wood, petroleum or earth-oil, honey, bees'-wax, and ivory. The mineral products are: iron, copper, lead, gold, silver, antimony, rubies, white statuary marble, limestone, and coal. In 1795, the quantity of teak and other timber imported to Madras and Calcutta, from the B. dominions required a return, chiefly in Indian and British cotton-goods, amounting to the value of £200,000; and this trade has been progressively on the increase since, as teak cannot be conveyed from the Malabar to the Coromandel coast unless at so great an expense as to preclude the attempt. This branch of commerce, before the late war, shared in the advantages of free trade: the B. were beginning to be clothed in British fabrics; and our merchants had pushed their enterprises to Amarapura, the capital. Sugar is manufactured by Chinese settlers. It is white and of good quality; but the exportation of it is prohibited. If this were not the case, and if encouragement were given to the manufacture, it might be carried to a great extent in B. The price of clayed sugar at Ava is 30 to 36 rupees the 100 vis, or 365 lbs. avoirdupois. The commerce betwixt the northern and southern quarters of the empire is greatly facilitated by the Irawaddi, on which several thousand boats are annually employed in transporting rice from the lower provinces, to supply the capital and the northern districts, also salt, and nappi, or pickled sprats. Articles of foreign importation are mostly conveyed up the Irawaddi; a few are introduced by way of Aracan, and carried over the mountains on men's heads. European broad cloth, hardware, coarse Bengal muslins, Cossimbazar silk handkerchiefs, china-ware, and glass, are the principal articles carried up the river. Merchants carry down silver, lac, precious stones, catechu, and some other articles. The lower parts of the Birman territory, the districts of Sarwan and Sarwadi especially, are considered as particularly adapted to the cultivation of indigo. The plant grows freely; and more than one indigo-factory was about to be established in B. by Europeans when the war broke out. The principal articles of import by sea into B., are Bengal, Madras, and British piece-goods, British woollens, iron wrought and unwrought, copper for sheathing, lead, quicksilver, borax, sulphur, saltpetre, gunpowder, firearms, sugar, arrack, and rum, a little opium, earthenware, Chinese and English glass-ware, cocoa-nuts and betel-nut. The trade in British piece-goods has of late years much increased, whilst that of Madras piecegoods has proportionally diminished. On the northern frontiers an active trade is carried on with China and other eastern states. The chief emporium of this trade is at a place called Bhanmo, and at Midai, 4 or 5 m. to the N of Amarapura. Mahommedan and Birman merchants from Ava go to Bhanmo to meet the Chinese traders, some of whom come down the river to Midai. The Chinese import copper, orpiment, quicksilver, vermilion, iron pans, silver, dried fruits, rhubarb, tea, honey, gold and silver thread, and raw silk. Their merchants travel on small horses and mules, and are said to be two months on the road. The tea which is brought by the Chinese is black, and is made up in round cakes or balls; some of it is of very fine flavour, and it is all of a different description from any that is sold in the market of Canton. The better qualities are well-adapted for Europe. The retail price is but one tikal—little more than a rupee—for one vis, or nearly 4 pounds. This tea is used by all who can afford it; but a cheaper sort, said to be the produce of some part of the B. territory, is an article of great and general demand. The returns of the trade with the Chinese are chiefly cotton, amber, ivory, and bees' wax, with a small quantity, perhaps, of British woollens. The quantity of cotton exported from B. is very considerable: it is estimated at not less than 70,000 bales of 300 pounds each. The greater part of it is cleaned. The Ava cotton of the lower provinces is of a short staple; but that of the upper is long, and of a fine texture. The cotton of Pegu, it is said, is sent to Chittagong and Dacca; and is the material of the fine Dacca muslins. Another line of traffic is that with the country of the Shans, or as it is termed by Europeans, the kingdom of Laos. The Shan traders repair annually, in the dry season, to the B. country, bringing with them sticklac, bees' wax, a yellow dyewood, various drugs and gums, raw silk, lacquered ware, ready made jackets stuffed with cotton, onions and garlic, turmeric, and coarse tea in cakes. The chief returns are dry fish, nappi, and salt. The chief fair at which the Shans attend is at Pelk, 6 or 8 m. S of Ava, on a small river which falls into the Irawaddi under the walls of the capital. There are several smaller fairs along the E bank of the Irawaddi; and one more considerable is annually held at the Dagon pagoda, near Rangoon. The Birmans, like the Chinese, have no coin: silver in bullion, and lead, being the current monies of the country. What foreigners call a tikal, properly kiat, is the most general piece of silver in circulation. It weighs 10 pennyweights, 103 grains. The subordinate currency is lead. Government. ] Government in B. is a coarse, rude despotism. The king is absolute lord of the life and property of his subjects: his will is law, and his voice fate. The wongees, [i. e. 'Great lords,'] or chief ministers of state, are considered as next in rank to the princes of the blood. They are four in number; and conduct, in the name of the king, the whole affairs of the nation. They are assisted by four woondocks, [i. e. 'Lord-helpers,'] who, in the council-hall, sit in a deliberative capacity, without being permitted to vote. Next to the woondocks are four attawoons, or ministers of the interior. They enjoy considerable influence, and have access to the king at all times; and from them he generally chooses his privy counsellors. The Birmans, in the regulation of their government, seem to have a predilection for the number four. All the officers of his majesty's several establishments are, or ought to be, in double pairs. Hereditary honours are unknown. The liberty of bearing umbrellas is the recognised mark of official dignity. White silk umbrellas can be used by the king only. The king's direct revenues arise from tribute, and tithes of the produce of the Crown lands, the mines, and the imports and exports. His unsettled revenues arise from confiscations, escheats, fines, donations, &c. Before the cession of Aracan, Tenasserim, and the other provinces taken possession of by the East India company, it was estimated to amount to 15 lacs of rupees per annum. Military and Marine force.] The military strength of such a country as that of the B., is not to be estimated by its mere numerical force: much depends on the skill and perseverance with which it may be kept together. The regular army is said to be inconsiderable; but, when it is thought necessary, every man is liable to do military service. The fidelity of the army is secured in a way which displays the tyrannical politics of eastern despotism: the wives and children of the soldier are detained as hostages, and should he desert or behave with cowardice, his innocent family must atone for his offence by being burnt alive. Yet it is believed that during the late war with the English, the whole B. army did not exceed 50,000 men. The arms of the infantry are bows, muskets, and sabres; but, with the exception of a few body-guards belonging to the royal family, they are not uniformly clothed, and their arms are generally in a bad condition. The bow and arrow, and a short sword called dah, having a blade of about a foot and a half in length, are weapons with the use of which the B. are perfectly acquainted, and they possess abundant courage to use them either in close or distant combat.—Their war-boats are generally from 60 to 120 ft. in length, but very narrow, and rowed or rather paddled by men who sit two abreast the whole length. Each is formed from the single trunk of a teak-tree, and carries from 50 to 60 rowers armed with swords and lances, besides about 30 soldiers armed with muskets. The prow, which is flat and solid, is generally mounted with a great gun. These boats proceed upon any attack with great violence, but from being low in the water, they are liable to be run down. The number of war-boats belonging to the B. empire is estimated at 500. History.] In Dalrymple's Oriental Repertory, the Birmans are called Boraghmans. In the Birman alphabet, published at Rome in 1776, the name is written Bomans. They call themselves Mranmas, pronounced Myanmas. Their native country is Ava Proper, and they were at one time subject to the king of Pegu; but in the 16th cent. this warlike people revolutionized the country by taking possession of Ava, and then of Martaban. The Birmans continued masters of the country till 1740, when a civil war broke out in the conquered provinces of Pegu, which was prosecuted on both sides with savage ferocity. In 1750, and BIRMAN EMPIRE. 1751, the Peguans, with the aid of arms imported by Europeans, and the active services of some Dutch and Portuguese adventurers, made head against their rivals; and in 1752, Ava, the capital, surrendered to them at discretion, while Dweepdee, the last of a long line of Birman kings, was taken prisoner, with all his family, except two sons who escaped into Siam. Flushed with conquest, Binga Della, king of Pegu, returned to his hereditary dominions, leaving the government of Ava to his son Apporasa; but scarcely had the viceroy entered on the management of affairs, when one of those extraordinary characters whom Providence sometimes raises up to change the destinies of nations, appeared on the stage. This was a Birman called Alompra, a man of obscure birth, known by the name of 'the huntsman,' and the chief of Monchabu, then a poor village. Having collected around him a few devoted followers, Alompra defeated the Peguan forces in small skirmishes; and acquiring confidence, and gradually attracting more followers, in the autumn of 1753, he suddenly advanced, and obtained possession of Ava. Defeating the king of Pegu in several subsequent engagements, he eventually carried the war into his territories, and in three months took his capital, which he gave up to indiscriminate plunder and carnage. He next turned his arms against Siam; but, during the siege of the capital of that kingdom, his career of conquest was suddenly terminated, in 1760, by a fatal disease, in the 50th year of his age, and 9th of his reign. Alempra was succeeded by his son Namdojee Praw, a minor; but Shembuan, brother to Alompra, acted as regent, and, on the death of his nephew assumed the crown. Shembuan prosecuted the war against the Siamese, and took their capital in 1766, but did not retain permanent possession of that country. In 1767, the rising empire was invaded, on the side of Yunnan, by a Chinese army, 50,000 strong, which advanced as far as a village called Chibu; but the Birmans cut off their supplies, and then destroyed the whole of them, except 2,500, who were sent in fetters to the Birman capital, where they became naturalized subjects. Shembuan subdued Cassay in 1774, and died in 1776. His son and successor, Chenguza, a debauched and bloody tyrant, was dethroned and put to death in 1782, in a conspiracy headed by his own uncle Minderagee, who took possession of the government. This prince was the fourth son of Alompra. In 1783, he sent a fleet of boats against Aracan, which he easily conquered. He then marched against Siam, where he met with some checks; and, finding himself unable to retain possession of the interior, was obliged to content himself with the dominion of its western coast, as far south as Mergui, including the two important sea-ports of Tavoy and Mergui, which were ceded to him by treaty in 1793. The first act of aggression on the part of the Birmese against the British government occurred in 1795. Three criminals having fled across the border, the Birmese hesitated not to violate our territory in pursuit of them; but the invasion was promptly repelled. The protection afforded by our government to the Mughs proved the next cause of discord. The tyranny exercised by the Birmese governor of Aracan drove multitudes of the inhabitants of that province to seek an asylum within our territory; and as early as the year 1799, two-thirds of the Mughs of Aracan are supposed to have exchanged the habitations of their fathers for a home and settlement under British protection. Jealous of these proceedings, a Birmese army of 4,000 men broke into the province of Chittagong, but soon afterwards fell back across the frontier. At this juncture it was imprudently resolved to settle the refugees permanently in the district between the Ramoo river and the Naaf, that is to say, in the immediate presence of their conquerors. The situation, indeed, seemed favourable to people of their habits, and the territory was without legal claimants, but the consequences were such as might have been anticipated. The Mughs formed themselves into bands of marauders, and kept up a system of incessant predatory incursions against their hereditary enemies in Aracan. On the accession of the Marquis of Hastings to the supreme government in India, he found an open breach with the Birmese all but effected; he managed, however, to hinder its occurrence, and so far humoured his neighbours as to permit a Birmese force to follow the Mugh depredators into the forests of Chittagong. But this indulgence only raised the demands of the Birmese, and all farther negociations with them were broken off. From that period (1814) up to 1824, the two parties stood towards each other in the situation of ostensible friends and secret enemies. At last, in consequence of certain insolent proceedings on the part of the Birmese, particularly in the unjustifiable arrest of a few European and American missionaries resident in the country, as well as the occupation of a district lying within the line of our possessions, it was determined in 1824 to declare war against them. A force of 6,000 men assembled at Port-Cornwallis, in the Great Andaman island, and sailed on the 4th of May for Rangoon, the principal sea-port in the Birman empire, the capture of which was effected with little opposition. But it was not until after near twelve months had been lost in a series of military operations of which the mouth of the Irawaddi was the base, that an invasion of Birmah was attempted from the maritime country of Aracan, which is divided from the kingdoms of Pegu and Ava Proper, by a chain of mountains running N and S, parallel and intermediate to the sea-coast and the course of the Irawaddi. It was projected, that a second invading force, entering Aracan, should cross the mountains and strike upon the Irawaddi, so as to form a junction with Sir Archibald Campbell's division; and an army of 10,000 men, under General Morrison, was assembled on the Chittagong frontier for that purpose. General Morrison, after a smart action, captured the city of Aracan, while Sir A. Campbell was advancing to Prome. But, though the routed enemy retired upon the Irawaddi, the passage over the mountains was believed to be impracticable; and all farther attempt at co-operation was abandoned. General Morrison was thus compelled to remain in "the swampy, pestilential flats of Aracan," where one-half of his army perished by disease, and the rest became so emaciated from sickness that his force was completely disorganized. Notwithstanding these misfortunes, a division of the army was despatched as far as Martaban itself, that is to say, 100 m. from Rangoon, which was captured; and Yek, situated between Martaban and Tavoy, shared the same fate. Having reduced the important post of Donnbu, the road to Prome lay open to Sir A. Campbell's division, which passed the rainy season very agreeably in that city. The first exploit which marked the opening of the new campaign was the total overthrow, in detail, of the Birmese army; whereupon an attempt was made to delude our general into the belief that his Birman majesty was willing to treat. The general, however, knew his adversary too well, and moved on towards the capital; and on the 8th of February the British columns arrived at Pagahm-mew, where they gave battle to the enemy's army, amounting to 20,000 men, and entirely defeated it. His Birmese majesty now submitted to the terms which he had so repeatedly rejected. On the 24th of February, a treaty of peace, between the East India company on the one part, and his Majesty the king of Ava on the other, was executed at Yandabu, within four days' march of the capital. Of this treaty we quote the following articles:- Art. IL—His Majesty, the King of Ava, renounces all claims upon, and will abstain from all future interference with, the principality of Assam and its dependencies, and also with the contiguous petty states of Cachar and Jyntia. Art. III.—To prevent all future disputes respecting the boundary-line between the two great nations, the British government will retain the conquered provinces of Aracan, including the four divisions of Aracan, Ramree, Cheduba, and Sandowey, and his Majesty, the King of Ava, cedes all right thereto. The Anoupetoumieou, or Aracan mountains, (known in Aracan by the name of Yeornabourg or Pokhengloung range,) will henceforth form the boundary between the two great nations on that side. Any doubts regarding the said line of demarcation will be settled by commissioners appointed by the respective governments for that purpose, such commissioners from both powers to be of suitable and corresponding rank. Art. IV.—His Majesty, the King of Ava, cedes to the British government the conquered provinces of Ye, Tavoy, Mergui, and Tenasserim, with the islands and dependencies thereunto appertaining, taking the Saluen river as the line of demarcation on that frontier. Any doubts regarding their boundaries will be settled as specified in the concluding part of article III. Art. V.—In proof of the sincere disposition of the Birmese government to maintain the relation of peace and amity between the nations, and as part indemnification to the British government for the expenses of the war, his Majesty, the King of Ava, agrees to pay the sum of one crore (£1,250,000) of rupees. Art. VII.—In order to cultivate and improve the relations of amity and peace hereby established between the two governments, it is agreed that accredited ministers, retaining an escort or safeguard of fifty men, from each, shall reside at the durbar of the other, who shall be permitted to purchase or build a suitable place of residence, of permanent materials; and a commercial treaty, upon principles of reciprocal advantage, will be entered into by the two high contracting parties. After the conclusion of peace, Sir A. Campbell, deeming it of the highest importance that the inlet from Aracan to the heart of Ava should be known to us, in case of another war, despatched Captain Trant, with a battalion of sepoys, and the elephants of the army, to explore the best route across the mountains, from Sembewghewn, on the Irawaddi, in N lat. 20° 40', to Aeng in Aracan, in N lat. 19° 53'. Captain Trant found "a superb road" across the mountains, which had been executed by the Birman government some years before, to facilitate the intercourse between Aracan and Ava; and which, as it was the channel of so great an inland trade as to be annually traversed by 40,000 persons, ought to have been as well-known to our authorities in India as the high route from Calcutta to Cawnpore. The whole distance from the Irawaddi to Aeng is only 124 m.; and the detachment, as well as the elephants, accomplished a march, which had been till then supposed impracticable, in eleven days. In 1837, the prince of Sarawaddi, brother to the late king, seized the throne, and removed the seat of government from Ava to Monchabu. Authorities.] Symes' Embassy to Ava.—Finlayson's Siam.—Crawfurd's Embassy to Ava.—Malcolm's Travels. - Colonel Franklin's Notes on B. - Asiatic Researches.—Asiatic Journal.—Judson's Account.—Baptist Periodical Accounts.

BIRMENSTORF, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Aargau, district of Baden, near the Reuss. Pop. 897.—Also a town in the cant. of Zurich, 5 m. W of Zurich, on an affluent of the Limmat. Pop. 960.

BIRMINGHAM, one of the greatest manufactur- I. ing towns in England, situated in the hund. of Hemlingford, in the NW extremity of Warwickshire, and on the borders of the counties of Stafford and Worcester; 521 m. by railway from Gloucester; 85 m. by railway from Manchester; 75 m. by railway from Chester; 109 m. NW of London through Coventry, by the coach-road, and <math>112\frac{1}{2}</math> m. by railway; and <math>97\frac{1}{4}</math> m. by railway, SSW of Liverpool. The town of B. is about 2 m. in length. The principal part of the town is situated on a hill, nearly in the shape of a horse-shoe, rising on the SE side, from the river Rea, 115 ft. in 1,000 yards, from the Brook on the N side, 70 ft. in 2,000; and from the toe to the heel, 165 ft. in 4,000 yards. This ridge is a part of the watershed which separates the basin of the Trent from that of the Severn; and upon it is the summit of two canals which form communications with the oceans on the opposite side of the kingdom, namely, the Worcester canal communicat ing with the Severn, and the Old Birmingham canal which is placed in connection with the Trent. This canal-summit is 460 ft. above sea-level; but the floor of St. Philip's church, nearly in the centre of the borough, is 475 ft. above low-water mark at Londonbridge. The Rea, which discharges itself into the Tame about 2 m. from Birmingham, and is one of the sources of the Trent, passes through the lower part of the town from W to E. The older part is in a low and damp situation; but the upper and more recent part is elevated, being approached by an ascent from every side except the north-west. The streets are lighted with gas, and upon the whole well-paved. The principal streets are generally wide, and well made; but the narrow streets, alleys, and courts, are neglected as regards paving, cleansing, and draining; and as the courts in the parish of Birmingham alone exceed 2,000 in number, while their inhabitants exceed 50,000, this state of things calls loudly for amendment. The minimum width allowed by the local act in all new streets is 42 ft. The town is in general well-supplied with water of excellent quality from several public spring wells; but that obtained from public pumps is often very impure. The main supply of water, however, is derived from the river Tame. It is lifted into a reservoir 252 ft. above the river, and presenting a surface of 747,000 square feet, by two 90 horse-power engines. From this reservoir it is led 2 m. to the town through a main artery. The houses are in general well-built, chiefly of brick; the more recently erected are in most instances faced with Roman cement and plaster. Some remarkably handsome private dwelling-houses have been erected within the last few years. The town is said to be at present extending itself in an equal proportion with London. The estate of Lord Calthorpe at Edgbaston, is in itself a town of no inconsiderable magnitude, and its annual rental as building-land considerably exceeds the sum paid for its purchase in the last century. The annual rental of B. was returned for assessment in 1826, at £239,407. The real property assessed in 1815 was £311,924; and to property tax for the year ending April 5, 1843, £940,173, whereof £668,000 was on houses, £158,000 on canals, £61,826 on railways, and £18,974 on lands. — The poor-rates in 1776 were £5,895; in 1803, £18,680; in 1837, £39,658; in 1842, £79,033. These returns are exclusive of Aston and Edghaston. Under the new representation act, this most important town, so long without a representative in the great councils of the nation, sends two members to parliament. The parishes of B. and Edgbaston; and the townships of Bordesley, Duddeston-cum-Nechels, and Deritend, are included within the elective boundary of the new borough. The constituency was 4,619 in 1839-40, 3 B and 6,129 in 1842-3. The area of the parliamentary borough is 18,780 acres. Inhabited houses in 1831, 29,656; in 1841, 36,121. Pop. in 1841, 182,922, of whom 128,202 were born within the county, 4,683 in Ireland, and 720 in Scotland; while 557 were of foreign birth. The pop. of B., with Ashton and Edgbaston, in 1811, was 85,755; in 1821, 106,722; in 1831, 146,986. B. is one of the most healthy of our large towns. The subsoil is dry and porous, the water generally of good quality, and the air is considered salubrious, notwithstanding the large number of manufactories and furnaces; Dr. Price indeed considered Birmingham the most healthy town in England, the rate of mortality on an average of six years, being only as 1 to 59, while in Manchester it was as 1 to 37, and in London as 1 to 31. The mortality, as given in the Registrar-general's returns for 1840-42, appears to be 2.7 per cent. The returns of the district-registrar present a lower rate of mortality; being in 1841, 2.45 per cent.; and in 1842, 2.48 per cent.
