B. originally consisted of but one parish,—that of St. Martin, a portion of which was separated, in 1715, for the erection of the parish of St. Philip. the diocese of Worcester; for all civil purposes, however, Birmingham is still considered as one parish. The prosperity of B. has arisen from the extensive manufactures carried on in it,—the abundance of coal and iron-ore in the neighbouring districts,—and the extent of its connections by inland navigation scribed it as inhabited by "smiths that used to make knives and all manner of cutting tools, and loriners that make bittes, and a great many nailours," and has participated in the fame of the Soho works, belonging to Messrs. Boulton and Watt, which, though in the county of Stafford, are within 13 m. of B. It began to flourish in the reign of Charles II., but the manufacture of fire-arms was not introduced till that of William III., since which period it has risen to be the great mart for muskets, swords, army-accoutrements, and machinery. During the last war, the government-contract for muskets alone averaged 30,000 per month; and in the 15 years between 1804 and the termination of the war, Birmingham furnished upwards of 5,000,000 of fire-arms! The number manufactured in the next 15 years (1814- 1828) was estimated at 3,000,000, and the demand for these articles up to the present time is perhaps less fluctuating than for any other product of industry. A proof-house has been established by act of parliament, where the excellence or imperfection of all gun and pistol barrels is proved by a heavy charge determined by the Board of Ordnance. If they stand this test they are marked by a stamp, to counterfeit which is felony, and the sale of unstamped guns is punishable by a heavy fine. Burke pronounced Birmingham "the toy-shop of Europe," but this designation is now by no means characteristic, for the articles of universal utility manufactured here far exceed those intended for ornament and show. Of the smaller articles of manufacture, buttons are the most important. The immensity of this apparently trifling business may be conjectured from the fact that the leading manufacturers keep each from 10,000 to 12,000 different sets of cut steel dies for livery, military, and club buttons. Pins, steelpens, small iron-ware, plated goods, jewellery, and toys of all descriptions, are also extensively manufactured. At the pin-works, 12,000 pins can be cut and pointed, and 50,000 pin-heads made from the wire, in an hour. Of the quantity of steel-pens now manufactured here some idea may be gathered from the fact that upwards of 40 tons of fine sheet steel have been consumed in a year by the principal maker of these light and delicate articles; and so greatly has the manufacture increased and risen in perfection, that while the article has been constantly improving, the price has been perpetually on the decrease, so that at present the best three-slit pens may be purchased at 8d. the gross. It has been calculated that about 800,000 gross, or nearly 10,000,000 of steel-pens, are annually made in Birmingham only, employing about 600 persons, and consuming about 80 tons of fine sheet steel! Factories for articles of a larger description are also numerous. Among these are iron and brass founderies, manufactories of steamengines, machinery of every description, and metallic hot-houses. There are also extensive glass-works for the manufacture of crystal as well as the coarser kinds of glass. In 1845 there were employed of the pop. of B. in Metals, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Glass, . . . . . . . . . B. has only a few manufacturers employing large capitals; most of them have only very limited capitals, and many of them are only agents or middlemen. Mr. Porter of the Board of Trade has correctly described the usual mode of conducting the business: "Almost all the small wares of the district are made by workmen who undertake, each one in his particular line, to execute orders received by the merchants and agents settled in the town. The profitable performance of their contract, however, calls for the employment of a cheaper kind of power than is at the command of men who, like these workmen, have little or no capital; and this course of business has opened here, as well as at Sheffield, a channel for the employment of money in the town, at once profitable to those who engage in it and advantageous to the small manufacturer. The plan alluded to is this. A building, containing a great number of rooms of various sizes, is furnished with a steamengine, working shafts from which are placed in each apartment or workshop, which is likewise furnished with a lathe, benches, and such other conveniences as are suited to various of the branches of manufacture for which the rooms are likely to be needed. When a workman has received an order for the supply of such a quantity of goods as will occupy him a week, or a month, or any other given time for their completion, he hires one or more of these rooms of sizes and conveniences suited to his particular wants, stipulating for the use of a certain amount of steam power. He thus realizes all the advantages that would accompany the possession of a steamengine; and as the buildings thus fitted up are numerous, competition on the part of their owners has brought down the charge for the offered accommodation to the lowest rate that will insure to them the ordinary rate of profit on the capital employed. Before the introduction of this system, the trade of Birmingham was for the most part carried on by men of large capital, who employed journeymen, and gave a considerable credit to the merchants who dealt with them. At present, those merchants themselves employ the workmen, who can give no credit, but receive payment in ready money at the end of every week for such part of their goods as they can deliver in a finished state; and in this way the profit of the intermediate dealer is saved." A considerable number of children, probably between 3,000 and 4,000, under eighteen years, are employed in the manufacturing processes chiefly in the houses of the artisans. With respect to wages, it is difficult to draw an average where the rates vary from 70s. to 21s., according to the skill of the workman or the demand for the article, but we shall perhaps, not be much in error when we estimate them at 25s. for men above twenty; women earning about 10s., and the children from 6s. to 9s. weekly. By a report made to the B. Philosophical institution, in October 1836, it appears that 169 steam-engines had been erected in this town from 1780 to that period, of which 17 had been erected in 1834, and 22 in 1835. The total horse-power was equal to 2,700 horses. Within the same period engines equal to 162 horses' power had become void or removed. Of those erected and estimated in horse-power, 275 were used for grinding flour; 1,770 for working metals; 279 for pumping water; 87 for glass-grinding; 97 for working wood; 44 for paper-making and glazing; 37 for grinding clay; 61 for grinding colours and chemicals; and 50 for sundry purposes. The estimated consumption of coals was 126 tons per day; estimated number of persons employed, 4,000 males, and 1,300 females; and the estimated amount of power hired out, equal to 450 horses. These estimates were confined to engines within the borough, and, of course, did not include the great Soho works of Boulton and Watt. Of the 1,770 horse-power employed in working metals, it was computed that 162 is used by iron-founders, first applied in 1788; 570 in rolling copper, brass, and other metals, first applied in 1790; 150 in drawing wire, first applied in 1808; 201 in iron-forges and wrought-iron mills, first applied in 1810; 74 in nail-cutting, first applied in 1813; 104 in screw-making, first applied in 1819; and 34 in drawing metal-tubes, first applied in 1822. The canals and railroads, by means of which Birmingham communicates with other parts of the island and with the sea, are numerous and important. The old canal, originally, as executed in 1768-9, little more than 22 m. in length from Birmingham to its junction with the Staffordshire and Worcestershire canal below Wolverhampton, now opens a communication through the Severn with Shrewsbury, Gloucester, and Bristol, and through the Trent with Gainsborough, Hull, and London. This canal has also a junction with the grand line running through the Potteries of Staffordshire, to Manchester and Liverpool; so that both the Irish sea and the German ocean are laid directly open to Birmingham traders. - The new Birmingham and Fazely canal provides a similar water-conveyance by Tamworth, Atherston, Nuneaton, and Coventry, to Oxford; and hence by the Thames to London. The London and Birmingham railroad, first surveyed in 1830, and ultimately constructed at an expense little short of £5,500,000— nearly double the original estimate—connects the metropolis with this great manufacturing town, and affords immense facilities to commerce. This line is 1121 m. in length, and passes near the towns of Coventry, Rugby, Weedon, Wolverton, Leighton, Tring, Berkhamstead, Boxmore, Watford, and Harrow; intersecting six of the most beautiful counties of England, viz., Middlesex, Hertfordshire, Buckingham, Bedfordshire, Northamptonshire, and Warwickshire. At Coventry, it sends off a branch-line about 9 m. in length to Leamington, which is thus brought within 27½ m. of Birmingham; and at Blissworth it sends off a branch-line to Northampton and Peterborough. In 1845 acts were obtained for a branch to Bedford, 16 m. in length, to run off from the main line near Bletchley; and for a line from Dunstable to join near Leighton-Buzzard, 7 m. in length.—By means of the Grand Junction railway, between B. and Warrington, communication is maintained with Walsall, Bilston, Wolverhampton, Penkridge, Stafford, the Potteries, Whitmore, Crewe, Hartford, Northwich, Warrington, Manchester, and Liverpool. Its whole course, to its junction at Warrington with the Liverpool and Manchester railway, is 821 m. An act was obtained in 1845 for consolidating this line with the Bolton and Leigh, the Kenyon and Leigh Junction, and the Liverpool and Manchester lines.-The Manchester and Birmingham Extension railway opens a communication between the towns of Stone, Rugeley, Lichfield, Tamworth, Atherstone, and Nuneaton, and between all those places and Manchester to the north; and in conjunction with the Birmingham and Derby railway affords a communication between Derby and other parts to the eastward, and the Potteries, Manchester, Liverpool, and Chester. The length of the main line of this railway is 54 m. 67 chains; and of the branch 7 m. 56 chains. An act was obtained in 1845 for connecting this line with the Sheffield and Manchester line near Guidebridge.—The B. and Derby Junction runs out of the London and B. railway at Hampton-in-Arden, and is connected with the North Midland railway at Derby. Its length is 38 m. 6 furs. 8 chains.—The B. and Gloucester railway runs from B. to Gloucester, by Broomsgrove, Droitwitch, and Cheltenham, a distance of 521 m., and is connected by the Western Union railway, with the Great Western railway at Swindon, as well as the Bristol and Gloucester railway direct to Bristol. A line is established by its means with the western part of England and the south part of Wales. There is a branch-line to Tewkesbury, 2 m. and 10 chains long, running from Aschurch to Highstreet, Tewkesbury. In 1845, the company obtained an act for a branch junction-line with the Midland railway at Aston-juxta-B. Monday, Thursday, and Saturday, are the marketdays. An excellently-arranged and spacious markethall, with extensive storing-vaults beneath, has recently been erected, in a simple, massive, and appropriate style of architecture. It is 365 ft. in length, and 108 wide. Fairs are held on the Thursday and two following days in Whitsun-week; and on Thursday and the two following days nearest to the 29th of September. Horses and horned cattle are sold on Thursdays, in a spacious area to the south-west of the town, called Smithfield. At these fairs considerable business is done in cattle, horses, and agricultural produce generally. At the Michaelmas fair such immense quantities of onions are exposed for sale as to give a name to the fair; that of Whitsuntide is most celebrated for its toys and shows. The public amusements of Birmingham are conducted on a very superior scale. The theatre-erected in 1821 on the site of one burned to the ground in the preceding year—is a handsome building, with a stone front in the Ionic order. The season generally commences in May and terminates in October.—The musical festival of B. occurs every third year, and generally attracts crowds of strangers. The townhall is a very splendid edifice of the Corinthian order, modelled on the temple of Jupiter Stator at Rome, and erected in 1833. The interior exhibits a saloon 140 ft. in length, 65 ft. wide, and 65 ft. high, calculated to be capable of containing about 3,000 persons seated, and at public meetings, when the forms are removed, upwards of 8,000. Here the musical festivals are held, and are aided by a magnificent organ. Machinery in union with steam-power was employed in cutting and fluting the columns and working the stones. The structure is of brick, faced with Anglesea marble, 166 ft. in external length, 104 ft. broad, and 83 ft. in height; the estimated cost was £18,000. -A Society of Arts was instituted here in 1814, and revived and re-arranged in 1821.—A library was established in 1798, which now contains 30,000 volumes; it is a handsome pile of building, containing, besides the spacious apartment in which the principal portion of the books is deposited, a circular reading-room, lighted by a dome-lantern resting on elegant Ionic pillars of porphyry. A new library on a similar principle, but on a smaller scale, has more recently been established.—A news-room was erected in 1825; it is a handsome structure, ornamented with lofty pillars of the Ionic order, and containing an appropriate suite of apartments. - The Philosophical institution, consisting of about 400 members, was <u>たたたたべたたんり プストルストスト トラルストイ グラン ドイトライン トリタイ フィック スティック アディ・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー ディー・ディー</u> established about 50 years ago; its plan was extended in 1810, and lectures on various branches of science are delivered in the theatre erected for the purpose; connected with this establishment, is a museum, at present chiefly rich in geological specimens, scientifically arranged; an extensive collection of philosophical apparatus; a library and reading-room.-A school of medicine and surgery was established here in 1828. A mechanic's institution has been in existence here since 1825; it numbers about 400 members. A second institution of nearly similar nature, under the name of the 'Athenæum,' was established in 1839, and is increasing. There are also several minor societies formed by persons of the industrious classes for intellectual improvement and rational recreation.—A public cemetery company was formed in 1832.—A horticultural society was established a few years ago. The conservatories are spacious, lofty, and well-filled with valuable specimens.—Barracks were erected at the NE extremity of the town in 1793.—The free grammar-school of B. is endowed with revenues arising from land feued for building, of which the rental, in 1839, was £5,000, and which is still increasing by the expiration of leases. It was founded in 1552 by Edward VI. The management of this school is vested in a bailiff and 18 governors, who appoint the masters and ushers. A new and suitable edifice was erected in 1834 by the governors of this wealthy establishment, on a convenient site in the centre of the town, at an expense of nearly £50,000. The design was furnished by Mr. C. Barry of London, and presents a mixed style of Gothic architecture, characterized by new but beautiful and appropriate combinations. The external form of the structure is quadrangular, and extends 174 ft. in front, and 125 ft. in flank; the internal arrangements embrace two courts similar in form, around and between which the different sections of the buildings rise. B. was not until recently incorporated; and to this probably it owes in part its rapid advancement, for there is no obstacle to the settlement of strangers here in any line of business. This town, without disposable public or corporate funds, has, within the last thirty years, by the spirited expenditure of individuals, and the judicious management of the street commissioners, changed its character of blackness, closeness, and defective taste, for that of a town of spacious, wide, and well-paved streets, abounding in public buildings, in the offices of large trading companies, and in private undertakings, exhibiting generally a highly advanced state of architectural decorations, and a general air of substantial wealth and independence, which is seldom equalled.—B. obtained a municipal charter on the 31st October, 1838. It is divided into 6 municipal wards. A separate court of quartersessions for the borough has recently been granted by the Crown. The proportion of known bad characters to the pop. is estimated as 1 in 67. There were, in 1840, 1,434 licensed victuallers in Birmingham: 385 beer-shops 'licensed to be drunk on the premises;' 30 'licensed to be drunk off the premises;' and 13 places licensed for the sale of cider: making in all, 1,885 places licensed for the sale of strong drinks. Every 22d house is so occupied, being one house to every 108 of the population. The average annual cost to each individual in Birmingham for intoxicating drink is £3 12s. 8d. The foundation-stone of a new borough-gaol was laid at Winson-green in October 1845. The style of the building will be castellated, built of brick with stone-facings. The gaol will be built upon the same principle as the modelprison in London, taking into consideration that in this prison there will be four classes of prisoners, while at Pentonville there is but one. There will be accommodation for 300 prisoners. BIR B. is mentioned in Domesday book, under the name of Bermengeham. The name, as well as those of the neighbouring hamlets of Castle and West Bromwich, is supposed to have been derived from the Saxon brom, - broom, which grows in the vicinity, and wick, a 'descent.' Some antiquaries suppose it to be the Bremenium of the Romans; others, amongst whom is the local historian Hutton, assert that it was a British town of some importance, and celebrated for the manufacture of arms, even previous to the Roman invasion. During the last four centuries it has been written Brumwycheam, Bromwycham, Bermichan, Bremecham, and Burmyngham, also with various transpositions of the vowels in the two first syllables. The early history of its present manufactures is involved in obscurity; but in the reign of Henry it is described by Leland as 'a good markettown,' the residence of 'smithes that used to make knives and all manner of cutting tooles; lorimers that make bittes and a great many nailowrs;' and about a century later we find it supplying 15,000 sword-blades to the troops of the Parliament, whose cause it warmly espoused against Charles I. and his nephew Prince Rupert. Bishop Gibson, in his edition of 'Camden's Britannia,' dated 1722, remarks that "most of the inhabitants be smithes and other artificers in steel and iron, whose performances in that way are greatly prized both at home and abroad." The editors of the 'Magna Britannia,' published in 1734, describing Birmingham, say: "The present state of this town is this: It is very populous, and swarms with inhabitants, but most of them of the meaner sort, which serve for carrying on the trade of ironworks, for which they are such ingenious artificers, that their performances in iron and steel are admired not only at home, but in foreign parts. We hear nothing here but the noise of hammers and anvils, which gain makes the sweetest music to the people of it. The lower part of the town is very watery, but the upper is stored with many handsome buildings." Some years later the manufacture of brass goods was introduced, and carried to some perfection by John Taylor, a celebrated brass-founder, to whom we owe the invention, or improvement, of the stamp and press. The art of japanning, both on metal and papier mache, was introduced about the same time by John Baskerville, to whose inventive genius, also, we owe improvements in typefounding and printing that raised him to the dignity of a second Caxton; and in 1764 were opened the far-famed Soho-works,— a vast manufacturing depot, brought to perfection by the united talents of Matthew Boulton and James Watt, under whose superintendence it sent forth almost every conceivable metallic commodity, from the copper penny of the realm, and innumerable articles of plate, bronze, and brass, to the ponderous steam-engine. Birmingham was the scene of a disgraceful political riot in July 1791, which commenced on the 14th, and continued several days, during which property was destroyed to the amount of £60,000, for which a compensation was granted by parliament to the amount of about £27,000, and some of the ring-leaders were executed. A somewhat similar outrage was perpetrated in this town, in 1839, by some misguided chartists, who set fire to several private buildings in the neighbourhood of the Bullring, and committed various gross excesses until dispersed by the appearance of the military.

BIRMINGHAM, a town of Pennsylvania, U. S., in Warrior Mark township, Huntingdon co., on the W branch of the Little Juniata, 107 m. WNW of Harrisburg. Pop. in 1840, 235. Lead and iron are found in the environs.—Also a township in Delaware co. Pop. in 1840, 605.—Also a township in Chester co. Pop. in 1840, 605.—Also a village of Ohio, in Florence township, Erie co., 115 m. NE of Columbus, on the W bank of the Vermillion.—Also a thriving village of Michigan, in Oakland co., 19 m. NW of Detroit, pleasantly situated on one of the head-streams of the Rouge.
BIRMINGHAM (NEW), a village in the p. of Kilcooley, co. Tipperary. Pop. 315.
BIRNA, a river of the Malay peninsula, in the prov. of Salangore, which falls into the straits of Malacca in N lat. 3° 50'.

BIRNAM, a mountain in Perthshire, near Dunkeld, rising from the S bank of the Tay to an alt. of 1,580 ft. It is nearly 12 m. SW from Dunsinane; and commands an extensive view of Strathmore, the Stormont, Athole, and Strathbraan. Shakspeare's tragedy of Macbeth has rendered this mountain classical ground in Scotland.
BIRNBACH, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Lower Danube, near the N bank of the Rott, 19 m. SW of Passau.

BIRNBAUM, or MICAZYCHOD, a town of Prussia, in the circle of the same name, in the prov. and 45 m. WNW of Posen, and 17 m. NE of Meseritz, on the l. bank of the Warta. Pop. in 1837, 2,637, of whom 760 were Jews. It contains a castle, a Catholic and a Lutheran church, a synagogue, and an orphan asylum; and has manufactories of linen and cloth, a cotton spinning-mill, and a tannery.—The circle of B. comprises a superficial area of 207 sq. m.; and contained in 1837 a pop. of 26,923. It is to a considerable extent covered with lakes, marshes, and turf-pits; but in some parts it is extremely fertile.
BIRNIE, or BIRNI (NEW), a town of W Africa, in the kingdom of Bornu, near the SW extremity of Lake Tchad, 20 m. S of Kuka, and 8 m. ESE of Old Birnie. Denham estimated its inhabitants at 10,000.

BIRNIE, or BIRNI (OLD), a town of W Africa, in the kingdom of Bornu, on a tributary of the Yeou. It once covered a space of 5 or 6 sq. m., and had 200,000 inhabitants; but it is now in ruins. Denham found the remains of its walls still standing, in large masses of hard red brick work, from 3 to 4 ft. in thickness; and from 16 to 18 ft. in height.
BIRNIE, a parish in Elginshire, on the Lossie. Area 5,784 Scots acres. Pop. 407.

BIRON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, and cant. of Monpazier, on a hill 27 m. SE of Bergerac. Pop. 1,124.—Also a town of Belgium, in the grand duchy of Luxemburg, 9 m. NE of Marche.
BIRONICO, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, in the dist. and 18 m. N of Lugano, in the valley of the Agno, at an alt. of 1,480 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 189.
BIROSLAF. See BERISLAV.

BIRR, a parish in King's co., Ireland. Area 7,217 acres. Pop. 9,567. It contains the town of B. or Parsonstown, which stands on the r. bank of the Little Brosna, 10 m. NE of Borris-o'-Kane. Pop. 6,336.

BIRRESBORN, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, and circle of Treves on the Kyll. It possesses mineral springs which are much frequented, and quarries of mill-stone. In the environs are the acidulous mineral springs of Brudelweis.
BIRRIM, a river of Upper Guinea, which takes its rise in the territory of Akim; directs its course WSW into Assin; and then curving WNW unites, after a course of 75 m., with the Busumpra or Chamah.

BIRS, or BIRSE, a river of Switzerland, which takes its rise in the Jura mountains, in the cant. of Berne, to the E of the pass of Pierre Pertuis; passes Moutier and Laufen, and entering the cant. of Basel, falls into the l. bank of the Rhine, 1 m. E of the cap., after a course from SW to NE of 45 m. Its principal affluent is the Sorne. The B. is liable to inundation, and sometimes devastates the districts through which it passes. Its waters abound with fish.
BIRS-E-NIMRUD. See Babylon.

BIRSA, or Birsay, a parish, united with Harray, in the N part of the mainland of Orkney, containing a superficies of about 35 sq. m., and 10 m. of seacoast. Its surface is hilly and undulating; and covered to a great extent with what is called lubba, a sort of coarse grass composed of carices, bent, and moor-grasses. Pop. in 1801, 2,176; in 1841, 1,634.

BIRSE, a large upland parish in the S of Aberdeenshire, bounded on the N by the river Dee, and intersected by three spurs of the Grampians, running in a NE direction from the main chain. The superficies probably exceeds 120 sq. m. Pop. in 1801, 1,266; in 1841, 1,295.

BIRSECK, an ancient castle of Switzerland, in the cant. of Basel, in the division of and near Arlesheim. The amt of B. is intersected by the Birs, and is one of the richest portions of the cant. It produces grain in great abundance, and excellent wine, and its pasturages are extremely luxuriant. It was united to Basel in 1815. BIS

BIRSIS, a village of Bengazi, in N Africa, in a fertile plain, 31 m. from Bengazi, and 7 m. from Teuchira, within 13 m. of the sea. A little to the SW of this place are ruins which Beechey is inclined to identify with the Neapolis of Ptolemy. The plain between the Cyrenaic range and the sea is here about 6 m. in breadth, and highly productive wherever cultivated.

BIRSK, a town of Russia, the cap. of a dist. of the same name, in the gov. of Orenburg, on the r. bank of the Bielaia, 50 m. NW of Ufa. Pop. 3,500. The dist. of B. is intersected by several ranges of low mountains, forming the broad valleys watered by the Biélaia, Ufa, Tanyp, and Birza. A great portion of its surface is covered with forests. The rearing of cattle forms one of the principal employments of the inhabitants.
BIRSTALL, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. SW of Leeds. Area 13,180 acres. Pop. 29,723. There are extensive wool-spinning mills in this p., and coal and iron stone mines.

BIRSTEIN, or BIERSTEIN, a town of Hesse-Cassel, in the prov. and 23 m. NE of Hanau, and circle of Saalmunster. It lies near a tributary of the Kinzig, and at the base of an eminence on which the castle of the princes of Isenburg-Birstein is situated. Pop. 870. Iron is found in considerable quantities in the environs.
BIRSTWITH, a township in the p. of Hampsthwaite, in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 71 m. W of Knaresborough. Area 1,670 acres. Pop. 676.
BIR-TEMAD, a town of Barbary, in the state and 140 m. SW of Tripoli, on the route from that town to Gadamis.

BIRTERBUY BAY, a fine natural harbour in co. Galway, opening between Masa point and Urrisbeg; 3 m. wide at its entrance, and penetrating the land to the extent of 8½ m. A profusion of islets and rocks stud the interior; and within the basin of the bay, or territory drained into it, are nearly 100 lakes and tarns.

BIRTHELM, or BERETHALEM, a town of Transylvania, in the circle and 10 m. ESE of Medgyes. It is the residence of the Lutheran superintendent of the prov. Pop. 3,290. The wine produced in the environs is reputed the best in the principality.
BIRTLE. See BAMFORD.

BIRTLEY, a chapelry in the p. of Chollerton, in Northumberland, 5 m. SE of Bellingham. Area, with Broomhope, 2,290 acres. Pop. 472.—Also a chapelry in Chester-le-Street p., in Cheshire, 5 m. S by E of Gateshead. Area 1,480 acres. Pop. 1,759.
BIRTSMORTON, a parish in Worcestershire, 6 m. SW of Upton-upon-Severn. Area 1,410 acres. Pop. 313.

BIRU, or Beru, a kingdom of Western Africa, in Sudan, bounded on the N by the Sahara; on the E by the kingdom of Timbuctu; on the S by the states of Massina and Bambarra; and on the W by the kingdom of Ludamer. It is said to be populous; and the cap., Walet, is reputed the greatest town in Nigritia. This state is, however, to a great extent still unknown. It has a Moorish government; but is subject to the king of Timbuctu.
BIRZE, or BIRJE, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Wilna, 36 m. NNE of Poneviej. It is the cap. of a small principality belonging to the family of Radziwil, and contains a fine castle. Pop. 1,600.

BISACCIA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Principato Ultra, in the dist. and 12 m. NE of San- Angelo-de-Lombardi, and 3 m. SW of Lacedogna. It is situated on a hill near the source of the Carapella, and contains several churches and an hospital. It is the cap. of one of the dioceses of the united bishopric of San-Angelo-de-Lombardi and Bisaccia. Pop. 5,916.
BISACQUINO, or Bussachino, a town of Sicily, in the intend. of Palermo, in the dist. and 7 m. SSW of Corleone. Pop. 7,150.
BISAGOS. See Bissagos.
BISAMBERG, a village of Lower Austria, in the circle and 1 m. ESE of Korneuburg, and 10 m. N of Vienna, near the Danube. It contains a castle. The environs produce excellent wine. Pop. 5,916.

BISAN, a monastery of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Tigre, and district of Hamazen, on the W acclivity of Mount Assali, 180 m. NNW of Antalo. It is now abandoned and falling into ruins. BISAN, Beisan, or Bysan, a village of Syria, in the pash. and 82 m. SSW of Damascus, on a tributary of the Jordan, 55 m. NNE of Jerusalem, at the base of Jebel Gilbo or Gilboa. It is on the site of the Bethsan of Scripture, the Scythopolis of the Greeks, the largest city of the Decapolis; and the outline of its theatre is still quite distinct.
BISANIG, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Sonora, on a lake at the head of the San Ignacia, 160 m. W of Arispe.
BISANTHE. See Rodosto.
BISARCIO, a once flourishing town, now in ruins, in the island of Sardinia, at the extremity of the plain of Ozieri, which gives its name to a bishopric of which it was the seat in the Middle ages.

BISATO, a canal of Venetian Lombardy, which issues from the Bacchiglione at Lungare, in the prov. of Cenone, runs in a SSW direction to Albettone, where it becomes navigable; thence entering the prov. of Padua, passes Vo, and unites with the Frassine 2 m. above Este. It is 29 m. in length. It was formed in 1365.
BISBAL (LA), a town of Spain, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. of Catalonia, 13 m. ESE of Gerona.
BISBROOKE, a parish in Rutlandshire, 2 m. E of Uppingham. Area 720 acres. Pop. 211.
BISCARA. See BESCARA.
BISCARI, a town of Sicily, in the Val di Noto, 20 m. NW of Modica. Pop. 2,700.
BISCARROSSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, in the cant. and 9 m. NW of Parentis-en- Born, on a marsh of the same name. Pop. 1,551.
BISCATHORPE, a parish in Lincolnshire, 8 m. NE by E of Wragby. Area 1,050 acres. Pop. 63.

BISCAY, or VISCAYA, a province of Spain, the principal of the three Bascongadas or Basque provinces; bounded on the N by the bay of Biscay; on the E by Guipuscoa; on the S by Alava and Burgos in Old Castile; and on the W by Burgos and Santander. It is of triangular form, and has a superficies of 1,216 sq. m. It is divided into the 5 partidos of Bermeo, Bilbao, Durango, Marquima, and Valmaseda. The pop. in 1833 was estimated at 111,433. It is surrounded on three sides by lofty sierras; and the Cantabrian chain spreads in several branches across it. Hence the surface is mountainous, and picturesquely diversified with deep narrow valleys, and small but well-cultivated plains; while some of the calcareous mountains have an easy slope, and are well-cultivated from the base to the summit. The summits of all the mountains, excepting a few which terminate in bare calcareous ridges, are crowned with forests, or covered with pastures; and the deep valleys are thronged with hamlets, iron-works, orchards, and gardens. Much of the timber is of recent growth, as the great consumpt in the numerous iron-works requires a constant supply of fuel to be provided; but there are still extensive natural forests, in which the wild holly, the oak, and the chestnut are of frequent occurrence; while furze, and several BIS species of erica, thickly intermingle themselves with the lower shrubs and brushwood. The vine is successfully cultivated in several districts. Wild boars and lynxes still exist in some of the forests, and game is everywhere abundant. - The chief river is the Nierva or Ibaizabal, sometimes also called the river of Bilbao, which is navigable to that town. The next in importance are the Cadaguo, the Mendaca, the Lequeitio, and the Ondarroa. Numerous streams of minor course rush down from the mountains into the bay of B., which here indents the coast with several small rias or bays, and breaks its heavy impetuous waves against rugged and bold promontories. The principal ports, in succession from E to W, are Hea, Bermeo, Pleucia, and Portugalete. The soil of B. is in general a stiff clay, heavy to work, and requiring a considerable admixture of calcareous matter for free production. Yet the inhabitants of this prov. convert their valleys, and the less rugged parts of their mountains, to excellent agricultural uses. The farmer has here not only to contend with a stubborn soil, and a climate occasionally severe for Spain, but with a rugged and frequently steep surface, on which animal labour cannot be used with the plough. Much is done by the hands of men, who even carry the manure up the rocky surface on their backs, and thus create a fertile soil in spots of very difficult access. The consumption of the lordship considerably exceeds its produce in grain. The only native productions with which B. supplies other countries are iron, chestnuts, and a little wool. Vineyards are numerous around Bilbao and Orduna; and a considerable quantity of an indifferent kind of wine, called chacoli, is manufactured. The trade and commerce of the country has, however, been paralyzed of late years by the war of contending factions, who have made the Basque provinces their grand field of contest. The intercourse of these provinces, within their own limits, and also with other parts of Spain, is greatly facilitated by excellent roads. Formerly the roads passed over mountains, or along the edge of precipices; and, in consequence of the inconvenience attending them, the three Bascongadas united to form new ones at the public expense. The language of the B. provinces is distinct from that spoken in the rest of the Spanish dominions, and its use remounts to a high period of antiquity. It is said to be soft, harmonious, and energetic; and so peculiar to the inhabitants, that Larramendi wrote a book called El imposible vencido arte de la langua Basconada. It is connected with the Celtic dialects, and has many words in common with the Latin. A proof of its high antiquity is supplied by the fact that the etymology of Peninsular geography may be best explained by it. The common Spanish dialect is not understood in the mountains. The Biscayans possess an inherent love of liberty, which nothing can prompt them to forego; and have jealously striven to preserve various privileges which they either enjoyed while an independent government, or obtained after becoming a province of Spain. Their taxations, instead of being duties imposed by the Crown, were voluntary contributions advanced by the inhabitants themselves. No stamped paper—which is one great source of revenue to the Spanish crown—was received in "the lordship;" and some articles, such as tobacco, elsewhere the subject of royal monopoly, were here open to the traffic of each individual. B. has no intendant; and foreign merchandise is subject only to anchorage duties. B. was not liable to the impress of seamen; it was exempt also from furnishing any quota in a levy of militia; nor could troops be quartered in the prov. Whatever related to its defence during war, or the preservation of tranquillity in the time of peace, belonged to its own inhabitants exclusively. The Biscayans being all noble, held a distinguished rank in the rest of Spain; and, excepting to the grand judge of B., who has his tribunal at Valladolid, were accountable to none other beyond the confines of their lordship. This, of all their privileges, is that which was guarded with greatest jealousy. Their laws and privileges equally removed them from the condition of most other subjects of the kingdom; for their affairs were determined by a general assembly of representatives, which was convoked every two years. These privileges were successively confirmed by the Austrian and the Bourbon dynasty in Spain; but the present government has greatly modified all the internal arrangements of the privileged provinces. The B. preserve a decided difference of character from the other Spaniards. They are of a gay and lively disposition, friendly, and hospitable; are faithful, active, and industrious; but, as if to counterbalance these good qualities, they are obstinate to a degree, irritable, and impatient. The women are equally active as the men; they participate in the most laborious employments, such as working in the fields, rowing boats, and carrying burdens on their heads, which require the strength of two men to lift up. "The wife yields not in strength to the husband, nor the sister to the brother;" and they share in sports elsewhere peculiar to men, such as tennis, in which they show themselves their successful rivals. The universal privilege of nobility produces a certain dignity among all the B. from the highest to the lowest; on proving that they originally belonged to the lordship, or have come in lineal descent from those who did, they are entitled to claim certificates of being gentlemen by blood. The three provinces have many ancient villas or seats, strong plain built edifices with square towers, which have existed from time immemorial: the owners of these are distinguished by the title of Hidalgos de Casa solar, or gentlemen of known property,—the most honourable appellation in Spain. The head of the family is called Pariente mayor, and is greatly respected by all the collateral branches. The origin of such Casas solares is thought to have been anterior to the establishment of Christianity in Spain; and before the use of archives or armorial bearings was known. But some of the owners are now so much reduced as to be under the necessity of cultivating their estates with their own hands; while branches of their families flourish in opulence in remote provinces. Simplicity of manners is one striking characteristic of the B.: the wives and daughters of the most wealthy do not disdain to engage personally in their domestic economy. A proverb current here marks the feelings of the people, La pobreza no es vileza, 'poverty is no blemish.' Extreme gaiety may be said to mark the temperament of B. They are passionately fond of dancing; and on holidays vast concourses assemble to dance under trees to the music of a rustic pipe and tabor. Children who die young, are preceded to the grave by musicians; and the body is crowned with roses. The same origin has been ascribed to the B. and the Irish. Guillermo Bowles, himself sprung of an Irish family, supposes that this is corroborated by the similarity of customs still practised among them. For example, the men and women of B. are extremely fond of pilgrimages. Collecting in troops, they journey from great distances to the churches of their tutelar saints, singing and dancing by the way to the sound of the tabor. The Irish do the same at the festivals of their patrons. The assemblies called guizones of Biscay, or the boulam keighs of Ireland, resemble each other, in the sudden and dangerous quarrels that often arise at them, and terminate without any remaining rancour, and without a deadly weapon being drawn. The people of both countries are extremely choleric; the least occurrence irritates them, and they cannot endure the most trifling slight; the chacoli of B., and the shebeen of Ireland, render both equally frantic and to be dreaded. The poor people in Ireland, as in B., eat from the same dish without forks, and using their fingers; and they dwell in the midst of smoke. The ancient Irish brogues are the shoes of B; "and the women here, as in Ireland, wrap a sabanilla or kerchief round the head, wear red petticoats, frequently go barefooted, carry weights on their heads, and labour along with the men. These, and other concurring circumstances,"-Bowles contends, "afford strong presumptions that the natives of the two countries have had one common origin." The modern B. consider themselves descended from the ancient Cantabri, who offered the most determined resistance to the Roman arms, and who were distinguished by the same energetic character which marks their posterity. The Romans finding themselves unable to conquer the Cantabrians by force, endeavoured to subdue them by famine; and so completely environed their retreats, that they were reduced to the utmost extremities, whereupon, it is reported, to avoid the slavery destined for them by their invaders, most of these brave people committed suicide; and Augustus having entered B., found it nearly a desert and partitioned it among his soldiers. Nevertheless, hostilities were frequently renewed by the survivors, until at length the Romans, in their turn, were overwhelmed by the irruptions of barbarous nations. B. was next assaulted by the Moors, who here met with a resistance equally resolute as that which had been offered to the arms of the Romans, and still more successful, for they were never able to penetrate its mountain-barriers. After a revolt in the 10th cent., we find the B. governed by a chief called Suria or Zuria, who, tradition says, was descended from the royal blood of Scotland. He constituted the provinces into an independent lordship, the sovereignty of which was enjoyed by his posterity, until subsequent revolutions ended in the lord of Biscay acknowledging the dominion of the king of Castile. In the 14th cent., Peter the Cruel, king of Castile and Leon, killed the lord of Biscay, usurped his possessions, and united them to his own; and since that period the kings of Spain have assumed the title of 'Lord of Biscay,' which is still retained.

BISCAY (BAY OF), that portion of the Atlantic ocean which is formed into a deep and wide bay by the western coasts of France and the northern coasts of Spain. Its two extreme points are, on the NE the isle of Ushant, in the bay of Brest, in N lat. 48° 28' 31", and E long. 5° 3' 19", which bears SSE 1/2 E true, 97 m. from BIS Scilly lighthouse; and on the SW Cape Ortegal, on the N coast of Galicia, in Spain, in N lat. 43° 46′ 40", W long. 7° 49′ 7". These two points are upwards of 300 geog. m. distant from each other; and the depth of the bay, measured by a line drawn from the mouth of the Gironde, perpendicular to the line joining the extreme points of the bay, measures nearly 260 geog. m. The character of the French coast bounding the bay, from Brest to the Gironde, is variable, presenting in some places a bold rocky outline; in others a comparatively low and level beach. To the S of the Gironde, as far as the mouth of the Bidassoa, it is very low, with extensive sand-downs. The Spanish coast is almost throughout bold, elevated, and rocky. The only islands belonging to the bay occur between Brest and the mouth of the Gironde. Belleisle, the NW end of which is in N lat. 47° 23', is the largest island in the bay.—The principal rivers which discharge themselves into the bay are the Loire; the Gironde, which is the common estuary of the Dordogne and the Garonne; and the Adour or river of Bayonne. On the coast of Spain no considerable river presents itself; but there are numerous and good harbours on this coast, formed by rias, or such small arms of the sea as are in Scotland termed lochs. The depth of water in this bay is generally great, and diminishes gradually towards the entrance of the English channel. The prevailing winds are from the NW, W, and SW. This circumstance, taken in connexion with the outline of its coast, which presents a wide opening to the full action of the waters of the Atlantic, which are allowed to roll in with their full and unbroken weight to the very extremity or broad bottom of the bay, where they are suddenly arrested and driven back upon the central masses of water, produces either a long heavy swell, or those high and short, but heavy waves, which are peculiarly disliked by navigators.—The current of the bay sets in from the NW of Cape Finisterre; sweeps along the coast of Spain at a distance of from 30 to 50 m.; and on arriving near the bottom of the bay turns N and NW, following the general outline of the French coast; whence it appears to be deflected by the NW extremity of the French main, and sweeps across the mouth of the English channel in the direction of the Scilly islands. It then bends further W, and approaches the coast of Ireland, whence it bends SW and S till it joins the N African current. This current has been called Rennell's current, in honour of that eminent investigator of the tides and currents of the ocean.
BISCAY (BAY OF), an extensive indentation of the S coast of Newfoundland, between Capes Race and Pine in N lat.
BISCAYNE, or BISCANO KAY, a low island near the SE extremity of the gulf of Florida, at the mouth of a bay of the same name, in N lat. 25° 43', and W long. 80° 5'.

BISCEGLIA, a town of Naples, in the prov. and 20 m. NW of Bari, in the dist. of Barletta. Pop. 15,141. It occupies a rocky promontory on the shore of the Adriatic, and is surrounded by walls. It is generally ill-built; but possesses a cathedral, 2 collegiate and several other churches, several convents, a seminary and an hospital. The supply of water is very deficient. Fairs are held twice a-year. B. is the seat of a bishop, suffragan of the archbishop of Trani. It has a port capable of receiving small vessels.—This town, the Natiolum of the Peutingerian tables, was restored in the eleventh cent. by Peter, count of Trani, one of the Twelve Norman leaders who conquered the kingdom of Naples. Its environs afford excellent wine.
BISCHBERG, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, 3 m. NW of Bamberg, at the junction of the Regnitz with the Mayn. It possesses a considerable trade in grain and pitch. Pop. 698.
BISCHDORF. See Guspoki.
BISCHEN-AM-BERG. See BISCHOFSHEIM.
BISCHEN-AM-HOHEN-STEG. See BISCHOFSHEIM (RHEIN).
BISCHHEIM, or Boeschen, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lower Rhine, and cant. of Oberhausbergen, 2 m. N of Strasburg. It possesses some starch manufactories. Pop. 2,347.
BISCHITZ, a market town of Bohemia, in the circle and 16 m. WSW of Jungbunzlau, and 20 m. NNE of Prague.

BISCHOFSBURG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of East Prussia, and regency of Königsberg, in the circle and 14 m. SSW of Rössel, on the Dimmer. It has a considerable commerce in thread and linen. Pop. in 1837, 2,248.

BISCHOFSGRUN, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, and presidial of Gefrees, on the Weisse-Mayn, at the foot of the Ochsenkopf, 15 m. ENE of Bayreuth. It contains manufactories of pitch and glass-ware. Pop. 546.
BISCHOFSHEIM, or BISCHEN-AM-BERG, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lower Rhine and cant. of Rosheim. Pop. 1,678.

BISCHOFSHEIM (NECKAR), a town of Baden, cap. of a bailiwick in the Neckar circle, 16 m. SE of Heidelberg, and 34 m. NE of Carlsruhe. It is surrounded by walls, and contains 2 castles, 2 churches, and a synagogue. It possesses manufactories of linen, and in the environs there are extensive paper and powder mills. The locality produces good wine and lint. Pop. 1,682.

BISCHOFSHEIM (RHEIN), or BISCHEN - AMHOHEN-STEG, a town of Baden, the cap. of a bailiwick, near the confluence of the Renchi with the Rhine, 18 m. WSW of Baden. It possesses a considerable trade in lint of local produce, and was formerly cap. of the county of Hanau-Lichtenberg. Pop. 1,744.

BISCHOFSHEIM (TAUBER), a town of Baden, cap. of a bailiwick in the circle of the Mayn and Tauber, on the r. bank of the Tauber, 16 m. SW of Wurzburg, and 45 m. ENE of Heidelberg. It contains a Franciscan convent, 2 churches, an hospital, and has several fairs and cattle markets. Pop. 2,338.

BISCHOFSHEIM-VON-DER-RHON, a town of Bavaria, cap. of a presidial in the circle of Upper Franconia-Aschaffenburg, on the Brend, at the foot of the Hohe-Rhone, 48 m. N of Wurzburg. It contains manufactories of linen, cloth, flannel, clocks, and a variety of articles in wood. Pop. 1,830. The presidial comprises, besides the city of the same name, 1 town and 36 villages, and 12,239 inhabitants. It presents a generally mountainous character. The culture of flax and the rearing of sheep form the staple industry of the district.
